paragraphs: Budget ~10 hrs/week: ~2 hrs read/watch, ~6 hrs coding, ~2 hrs writing (a README or short post — this is where it snaps into your head). The single biggest predictor of retention is the build-to-theory ratio.
The pattern that works for engineers like you:

Two cadences: the build phase (Weeks 1–11) is hands-keyboard, capstone-driven — don't stall on theory. The depth phase (Weeks 12–19) is what makes you senior in AI, not just functional: it's anchored on Chip Huyen's AI Engineering (O'Reilly) — keep the book open as the spine for W5–17, then AI system design (W17) and interview prep (W21) close it out.
Don't fall for: tutorial hell, framework-collecting, chasing every model release. Pick one frontier API (Anthropic) + one open-source path (HuggingFace + Ollama) and stick to them.
bullets: Read the topic intro (15 min) — skim, don't deep-read.
Build the smallest thing that demonstrates the concept, with no framework. Highest-leverage step.
Rebuild with the framework — now you see what it was hiding.
Write a 200-word note: "what I'd use this for at work."

items: Editor: VS Code + Python/Jupyter extensions, or PyCharm if you want JetBrains-familiar.
API keys: Anthropic ($5 free), OpenAI ($5), Groq (free tier, fast inference for experiments).
Local models: install Ollama, pull llama3.2:3b and qwen2.5:7b.
Vector DB: start with Chroma (in-process, zero-ops). Move to pgvector/Qdrant later.
Observability: sign up for Langfuse (open-source, self-hostable).
Git: one repo ai-learning-lab, one folder per week.
Claude Code: install Claude Code now — Week 2 turns it into your daily driver.

timeSummary.deliverable: MLflow + Langfuse prompts + HF Hub + DVC + CI eval gate
Capstone on AWS, IaC, SaaS, eval-gated

paidCourses: AI Evals for Engineers & PMs (Hamel Husain & Shreya Shankar) — highest-signal paid course in the space (~$2k). Pairs with Weeks 13 & 14.
Ed Donner — AI Engineer Core Track (~$15–20 on sale) — backbone for Weeks 3, 6, 11.
AI Engineer Agentic Track (~$15–20) — 8 projects across OpenAI SDK / CrewAI / LangGraph / AutoGen / MCP; drives Weeks 8–9.
AI Engineer Production Track (~$15–20) — AWS Bedrock/SageMaker, Lambda, Terraform, CI/CD, serverless multi-agent; drives Week 19.
notes.buyingTip: Buying tip: all three Ed Donner courses are on Udemy — the Personal Plan unlocks everything and beats buying individually; otherwise wait for the recurring ~$10–20 sale and never pay sticker. Chip Huyen's AI Engineering (the book you have) is the depth-weeks spine — no course replaces it.
pitfalls: You now own overlapping courses — don't binge them linearly. Use the resource map: one primary per week, the rest as reference. Watching all six Ed Donner courses + ByteByteGo + Cohen end-to-end is hundreds of hours and mostly redundant.
Don't let the depth weeks block shipping. The capstone (W16) is the hireable artifact; the later depth weeks deepen you but a portfolio project beats half-read theory. Ship first.
Don't chase "the correct framework." Week 9 is breadth on purpose — learn all four, commit to none.

Your backend rigor is a superpower past Week 8. Most AI engineers can't reason about throughput, idempotency, retries, or transactional semantics — your capstone and Week 18 should be 10× more production-ready than the median learner's.
Don't fine-tune unless evals say to. The #1 trap for technical learners.

Your edges are Weeks 4, 10, and 17. JVM-native AI + Temporal orchestration + inference optimization. Build the capstone on the JVM and write the blog posts — they're your differentiated interview assets.

weeks.title: Python Pre-Prep & Environment
weeks.goal: Goal: stop fighting Python. Be fluent enough that the language is invisible by Week 2. No AI yet — pure language + tooling delta from Kotlin.

days.focusSub: Syntax map, type hints, the traps

resources.label: Real Python — Python for Java devs
mCoding — 25 nooby Python habits to ditch

days.hands: Rewrite a Kotlin CSV transformer in Python with full type hints. Exercism Python: 3 starter exercises.

exercise.summary: You'll port a small program you already wrote in Java or Kotlin into idiomatic, fully type-hinted Python — proving you can read and write Python before any AI work begins. For example, reimplement a CSV transformer and annotate every signature, then run mypy --strict until it's clean. The point isn't the program; it's building reflexes in the language nearly every AI library speaks. Beginner trap: writing "Java in Python" (manual loops, getters/setters) instead of leaning on Python's lighter idioms — the next exercise fixes exactly that.
exercise.goal: Port a real Kotlin utility to idiomatic, fully-typed Python.
steps: Pick a small Kotlin/Java util you've written (e.g. a CSV transformer) and reimplement it in Python 3.12 with type hints on every signature.
Run mypy --strict and fix every error.
Do 3 starter exercises on the Exercism Python track to drill syntax.
acceptance: mypy --strict passes with zero errors.
The port handles the same edge cases as the original and is committed to week-01.
exercise.stretch: Add a pytest parametrized test covering 3 edge cases.

days.hands: Exercism: 4–5 exercises hitting comprehensions, dicts, generators.

exercise.summary: This swaps Java boilerplate for the Python idioms you'll see in every codebase: @dataclass instead of getter/setter classes, comprehensions and generators instead of loop-and-append, and with blocks for cleanup. Concretely, rewrite one data class as a frozen @dataclass and turn one loop into a comprehension. Knowing these makes library code readable and your code Pythonic. Watch out: comprehensions that grow three lines long are usually clearer as a normal loop — idiomatic doesn't mean cramming everything onto one line.
exercise.goal: Internalise the Python idioms that replace Java boilerplate.
steps: Rewrite a data-holding class as a @dataclass(frozen=True) with type hints.
Convert one loop-and-append into a comprehension, and one into a generator.
Wrap a resource in a with context manager.
acceptance: A generator is used to stream rather than build a list.
4–5 Exercism exercises on comprehensions/dicts/generators pass.
exercise.stretch: Compare generator vs list memory with tracemalloc.

resources.label: Real Python — Async IO in Python

days.hands: Async httpx fetcher hitting 5 URLs concurrently; time it vs sequential.

exercise.summary: Most AI work is I/O-bound — you spend each call waiting on a model API — so asyncio lets you fire many requests at once instead of one at a time. You'll write an async fetcher that hits 5 URLs with asyncio.gather, time it against the sequential version, and confirm one failure doesn't sink the batch. Expect a real speedup because the waits overlap. Beginner trap: calling a blocking (non-async) library inside async code freezes the whole loop — every step in the chain has to be awaitable.
exercise.goal: Get asyncio muscle memory and prove the speedup.
steps: Write an async httpx fetcher hitting 5 URLs with asyncio.gather.
Write the sequential version and time both.
Make one URL fail and ensure the batch still completes.
acceptance: The concurrent version is measurably faster than sequential.
A failing URL doesn't crash the whole batch; mypy is clean.
exercise.stretch: Add an asyncio.Semaphore(2) to cap concurrency and observe the timing change.

days.focusSub: Your structured-output validator all course long
days.hands: Model a billpay Payment as a Pydantic model with validators + a failing-validation test.

exercise.summary: Pydantic is a library that validates data against a typed schema and raises a clear error when it doesn't fit — the backbone of reliable model output later. You'll model a Payment, add field and model validators, and write a test proving a bad payload raises ValidationError; uv manages the project with a committed lockfile. This is the validator you'll reuse all course to force model output into a known shape. Pitfall: catching the ValidationError and silently continuing — the whole point is to fail loudly on bad data.
exercise.goal: Build the Pydantic structured-output validator you'll reuse all course.
steps: Model a billpay Payment as a Pydantic v2 model with a field validator and a model validator.
Write a test asserting a bad payload raises ValidationError.
Manage the project with uv + a committed lockfile.
acceptance: Valid payload parses; invalid raises a clear error.
uv.lock is committed.
exercise.stretch: Export the model's JSON Schema and adapt it into an LLM function/tool spec.

days.focusSub: Your Quarkus comfort zone, in Python
days.hands: FastAPI service: 2 endpoints (1 async) + 1 pytest. Commit to ai-learning-lab/week-01.

exercise.summary: FastAPI is the standard Python framework for serving models and agents behind an HTTP endpoint; pytest is how you prove it works. You'll build an app with two endpoints (one async), attach Pydantic request/response models, and write a test with TestClient. Nearly every project you ship this course is a FastAPI service, so this is your comfort zone. Beginner trap: forgetting async def on an endpoint that awaits I/O — it still runs, but blocks other requests under load.
exercise.goal: Stand up your Python service comfort zone (FastAPI + pytest).
steps: Build a FastAPI app with two endpoints, one of them async.
Add Pydantic request/response models.
Write one pytest using TestClient.
acceptance: Both endpoints return the correct shapes.
pytest is green and the service is committed to week-01.
exercise.stretch: Add an exception handler that returns RFC-7807-style problem details.

weeks.udemy: Udemy: none — courses assume Python. Don't pay for syntax.
weeks.deliverable: Async FastAPI service + Pydantic models, typed, tested.

weeks.title: Claude Code & Agentic Coding
weeks.goal: Goal — front-loaded on purpose. You already use Claude Code daily; this week turns it into an autonomous coding system you drive efficiently, then pushes into multi-agent coding. Spine: Ed Donner — AI Coder. You'll use these skills to build every later project, so the payoff compounds.

days.hands: Write a real CLAUDE.md for your Billpay repo: build/lint/test commands, module map, conventions (Exposed, Temporal, KSP). Run one real task end-to-end.

exercise.summary: CLAUDE.md is a file that tells an AI coding agent how your repo works — build/test commands, the module map, your conventions — so it stops guessing. You'll write one for a real repo, run a task driven only by that file, then delete whatever the agent never used. Think of it as onboarding docs an agent actually reads every time. Pitfall: dumping your whole wiki in — an overstuffed file wastes context and buries the rules that matter; keep it lean.
exercise.goal: Turn CLAUDE.md into a real, working project constitution.
steps: Write a CLAUDE.md for your Billpay repo: build/lint/test commands, module map, conventions (Exposed, Temporal, KSP).
Run one real task end-to-end driven only by that file.
Trim anything Claude didn't actually use.
acceptance: Claude completes the task using only the documented commands/conventions.
The file is under ~1 page and committed.
exercise.stretch: Add an AGENTS.md pointer and confirm both stay in sync.

days.hands: Create 2 skills in .claude/skills/: one to generate Docusaurus pages from KSP variant metadata, one /pr-summary. Invoke both.

exercise.summary: Skills and slash commands package a repeatable workflow into something an agent can invoke on demand — like a /pr-summary command or a doc generator. You'll build two real ones, run them on actual inputs, and document each one's trigger. This turns "paste the same long prompt again" into a one-word call. Watch out: making a skill too broad ("do everything") — narrow, single-purpose skills are far more reliable to trigger.
exercise.goal: Build reusable skills + slash commands for your workflow.
steps: Create two skills in .claude/skills/: one to generate Docusaurus pages from KSP variant metadata, one /pr-summary.
Invoke both on real inputs.
Document each skill's trigger and inputs.
acceptance: Both skills run and produce correct output on a real repo.
Each has a clear description so it triggers reliably.
exercise.stretch: Add a third skill that scaffolds a Temporal activity from a template.

days.focusSub: Run 100% of the time, unlike CLAUDE.md
days.hands: Add a PreToolUse hook that blocks force-push to main + scans writes for secrets (exit 2 = deny), and a PostToolUse hook running ktlint/detekt after edits. Verify they fire every time.

exercise.summary: Hooks run your own deterministic code at fixed points in an agent's loop — so a formatter, linter, or branch check fires every time, not just when the model remembers. You'll add a hook that lints edited files and one that blocks commits to a protected branch, and verify each fires. The lesson: put guarantees in code, not in prompts. Beginner trap: relying on the model to "please run the tests" — only a hook makes it non-optional.
exercise.goal: Add deterministic guardrails with hooks.
steps: Add a PreToolUse or PostToolUse hook that runs your formatter/linter on edited files.
Add a hook that blocks commits to a protected branch.
Verify each fires on the relevant action.
acceptance: The format/lint hook runs 100% of the time on edits.
The protection hook actually blocks the disallowed action.
exercise.stretch: Wire a hook that runs the fast test subset before allowing a push.

resources.label: AI Coder — Wk3 (Agent Teams, Ralph Loops)

days.hands: Run 3 subagents concurrently to review auth, payments, migrations. Use plan mode for a multi-file refactor; try a Ralph Loop (persistent self-correcting agent) on a sandboxed task; roll back via checkpoint.

exercise.summary: When a task is too big for one context window, you split it across subagents working in parallel and checkpoint long jobs so they can resume. You'll spawn 2+ subagents on isolated parts of a task, run a checkpointed loop on a multi-step refactor, and note where isolation helped versus hurt. The payoff is speed and focus on large work. Pitfall: parallelizing tasks that actually share state — isolated agents can make conflicting edits that don't merge cleanly.
exercise.goal: Parallelise with subagents, Agent Teams, and checkpointed loops.
steps: Spawn 2+ subagents to work isolated parts of a task in parallel.
Run a Ralph-style loop with checkpoints on a multi-step refactor.
Capture where context isolation helped vs hurt.
acceptance: A task is completed by parallel subagents with merged output.
A checkpointed loop recovers from an intentional mid-run failure.
exercise.stretch: Measure wall-clock of parallel vs serial on the same task.

days.hands: Run claude -p headless in a GitHub Action / pre-commit. Wire an autonomous loop: raise a Jira issue → agent builds, tests, opens a PR. Connect a GitHub MCP server; skim the Agent SDK + Cowork.

exercise.summary: Headless mode runs an AI coding agent non-interactively — in a script or continuous-integration pipeline — and the Agent SDK (Software Development Kit) lets you embed one in your own program. You'll run a scripted task headless, add a job that does an autonomous PR-review pass, and call the SDK from a small script. This is how agentic coding scales beyond your terminal. Watch out: letting an autonomous loop run without a cost/step cap — unattended agents need a hard stop.
exercise.goal: Drive Claude Code headless / in CI and via the Agent SDK.
steps: Run Claude Code headless on a scripted task (non-interactive).
Add a CI job that runs an autonomous PR-review pass.
Call the Agent SDK from a small script.
acceptance: The headless run produces the expected artifact with no manual input.
The CI job comments on a PR automatically.
exercise.stretch: Prototype an autonomous Jira→PR loop demo end-to-end.

weeks.udemy: Udemy: AI Coder (spine) + official docs.
weeks.deliverable: Billpay repo: CLAUDE.md + 2 skills + 2 hooks + CI PR-review + autonomous Jira→PR loop demo.

weeks.title: Gen AI Building Blocks
weeks.goal: Goal: understand how LLMs actually work, and ship 3 LLM-powered scripts from raw API calls — no framework.

days.hands: Note the $/M-token cost of 3 models you'll use. Sketch when each fits.

exercise.summary: A Large Language Model (LLM) is a system that predicts text one token at a time, and your first job is intuition for what each model costs and is good at. You'll tabulate price per million input/output tokens for a frontier, a mid, and a local model, then estimate the cost of a typical 2k-in / 500-out call for each. Knowing this stops you reaching for the biggest model by reflex. Beginner trap: comparing only the headline price — a cheap model that needs three retries can cost more than one strong call.
exercise.goal: Build cost/behaviour intuition for the models you'll use.
steps: Tabulate $/M input+output tokens for 3 models (frontier, mid, local).
Sketch when each model is the right pick.
Estimate the per-call cost of a 2k-in / 500-out request for each.
acceptance: A cost table covers 3 models with input+output pricing.
Each model has a one-line 'use when' rationale.
exercise.stretch: Add latency expectations (TTFT) per model from a quick probe.

days.hands: Tokenize 3 sample inputs; predict then verify token counts.

exercise.summary: Models don't see characters or words — they see tokens, sub-word chunks produced by a tokenizer, and that quirk explains a lot of odd behaviour and cost. You'll tokenize code, prose, and a non-English string, predicting counts before checking, and find a case where casing or whitespace changes the count. It makes pricing and context limits concrete. Pitfall: assuming one word equals one token — code, emoji, and other languages can be far more token-hungry, blowing past context windows you thought were safe.
exercise.goal: Understand tokenization well enough to predict odd behaviour.
steps: Tokenize 3 sample inputs (code, prose, a non-English string) and predict counts before checking.
Find an example where whitespace/casing changes the token count.
Note the cost implication of your worst case.
acceptance: Predicted vs actual token counts are compared for 3 inputs.
You can explain one concrete 'why did the prompt behave oddly' case.
exercise.stretch: Compare two tokenizers (e.g. GPT-4o vs Llama) on the same input.

days.hands: Work notebooks ch.1–6. Build 1: CLI that summarizes a dir of markdown notes (raw anthropic SDK).

exercise.summary: This is your first real script against a raw model API, with deliberate prompting instead of trial and error. You'll work Anthropic's prompt-engineering tutorial, build a command-line tool that summarizes a folder of notes via the raw SDK, then compare a few-shot version against a chain-of-thought one. Seeing the output change with the prompt builds the core skill. Beginner trap: blaming the model for a bad answer that was really a vague prompt — be specific about format, role, and constraints first.
exercise.goal: Ship your first raw-API script with solid prompting.
steps: Work Anthropic's prompt-eng tutorial notebooks ch.1–6.
Build a CLI that summarizes a directory of markdown notes using the raw anthropic SDK.
Add a few-shot example and a chain-of-thought variant; compare outputs.
acceptance: The CLI summarizes real files from a folder.
Few-shot vs zero-shot difference is documented.
exercise.stretch: Add a --style flag that switches system prompts.

days.hands: Build 2: extractor — job description → {role, seniority, skills[], remote} as a Pydantic model.

exercise.summary: Free-text answers are hard to use in code; function calling forces the model to return data in a schema you define. You'll build an extractor that turns a job description into a typed {role, seniority, skills[], remote} object, using the model's tool-calling to enforce the shape, and validate three messy inputs. This is how you bolt a model onto real software. Pitfall: trusting the schema blindly — always validate the result, because a model can emit a field that's the right type but wrong.
exercise.goal: Get reliable structured output via function calling.
steps: Build an extractor: job description → {role, seniority, skills[], remote} as a Pydantic model.
Use the model's tool/function-calling to force the schema.
Feed 3 messy inputs and validate every result.
acceptance: All 3 inputs produce schema-valid objects.
A malformed model response is caught and retried or surfaced.
exercise.stretch: Add a confidence field and abstain when low.

days.hands: Build 3: LLM that can call 3 tools — get_weather, search_files, run_sql.

exercise.summary: Tool use lets a model call your code — fetch weather, search files, run SQL — and fold the result into its answer; you'll build the dispatch loop by hand to see the mechanics. Give the model three tools, route whichever it picks, return the result, and handle both a tool error and a turn that needs no tool. Everything labelled "agent" later is this loop plus memory. Beginner trap: crashing on a tool error instead of returning the error text to the model — it can often recover if you let it see what went wrong.
exercise.goal: Implement a multi-tool dispatch loop by hand.
steps: Give the LLM 3 tools — get_weather, search_files, run_sql.
Write the dispatch loop that routes tool calls and returns results.
Handle a tool error and a no-tool-needed turn.
acceptance: The model correctly selects and calls the right tool per query.
Tool errors are returned to the model rather than crashing.
exercise.stretch: Add parallel tool calls in a single turn and merge results.

weeks.udemy: Udemy: Core Track — Week 2
weeks.deliverable: Repo week-03-basics + README on tokens, context windows, cost math.

weeks.title: JVM-Native AI — Spring AI & LangChain4j
weeks.goal: Goal — your unfair advantage. Re-implement what you just learned in Python (LLM APIs, tool use, structured output) in your real stack — LangChain4j / Spring AI on Quarkus — so you can ship AI into Billpay without leaving the JVM. Almost no AI-engineering learner can do this; you already own the stack. On-ramp: Java Brains — From Java to AI (skim if any concept feels shaky).

days.focusSub: Spring AI vs LangChain4j vs Quarkus-LangChain4j

resources.label: Java Brains — From Java to AI

days.hands: Add quarkus-langchain4j to a Quarkus project; wire an Anthropic/OpenAI chat model; get your first completion from Kotlin.

exercise.summary: If your background is Java/Kotlin, you can do AI on the Java Virtual Machine (JVM) instead of switching to Python — this picks that stack deliberately. You'll spin up a Quarkus project with Quarkus-LangChain4j, wire one chat call end to end, and write a one-pager comparing LangChain4j, Spring AI, and Quarkus-LangChain4j. Choosing on purpose beats cargo-culting a tutorial's stack. Pitfall: picking a framework for its star count rather than your runtime and team — the one-pager forces a real reason.
exercise.goal: Pick your JVM AI stack deliberately.
steps: Spin up a Quarkus project with Quarkus-LangChain4j (and skim Spring AI).
Wire one chat call end-to-end.
Write a 1-pager: LangChain4j vs Spring AI vs Quarkus-LangChain4j for your use.
acceptance: A JVM chat call returns a completion.
The decision doc states which you'll use for the capstone and why.
exercise.stretch: Note the native-image implications for each option.

resources.label: Java Brains — From Java to AI

days.hands: Port your W3 summarizer to a LangChain4j AiServices interface; add a system prompt + temperature; stream the response.

exercise.summary: LangChain4j's AiServices turns a chat model into a typed Java interface — you declare a method, it handles the prompt plumbing. You'll define an interface with a prompt template, stream tokens to the console, and swap the model provider via config alone. The provider-swap proves your code isn't welded to one vendor. Watch out: hard-coding the model name or key in code instead of config — you lose exactly the portability this exercise teaches.
exercise.goal: Use AiServices for typed chat, prompts, and streaming.
steps: Define an AiService interface with a prompt template.
Add parameters and stream tokens to the console.
Swap the model provider via config only.
acceptance: A typed AiService returns a completion from a template.
Streaming works and the provider is config-swappable.
exercise.stretch: Add a system message that enforces a persona and test it.

days.focusSub: map LLM output → Kotlin data classes

resources.label: Java Brains — From Java to AI

days.hands: Rebuild your W3 extractor to return a typed Kotlin data class directly — no manual JSON parsing.

exercise.summary: On the JVM you can have the model return a real Kotlin data class, not a string you parse by hand. You'll map output to a data class, validate a required field, and test what happens when the model returns malformed output. Type safety catches bad model output at the boundary. Pitfall: assuming structured output always parses — leave a tested fallback path for the day the model returns something off-schema.
exercise.goal: Map LLM output to Kotlin data classes safely.
steps: Have the AiService return a Kotlin data class (structured output).
Add validation for a required field.
Test a malformed-output path.
acceptance: LLM output deserializes into the data class.
A bad output is caught and handled, not propagated raw.
exercise.stretch: Generate the schema from the data class to constrain the model.

days.focusTitle: Tool calling & RAG on the JVM

resources.label: Java Brains — From Java to AI

days.hands: Expose 2–3 @Tool methods (incl. a read-only Oracle query); build a minimal RAG over Temporal docs using a pgvector embedding store.

exercise.summary: This does tool-calling plus Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) natively in Java — the model both calls your methods and answers from a document store. You'll expose two @Tool methods, stand up a pgvector store with a small corpus, and answer a question needing both retrieval and a tool call. It proves the JVM is a first-class AI runtime. Beginner trap: retrieving documents but never checking they're relevant — irrelevant chunks in the prompt produce confident wrong answers.
exercise.goal: Do tool-calling + RAG natively on the JVM.
steps: Expose 2 @Tool methods the model can call.
Stand up a pgvector embedding store and ingest a small corpus.
Answer a question that requires retrieval + a tool call.
acceptance: The model calls a @Tool method and uses retrieved context.
Citations/sources are returned with the answer.
exercise.stretch: Add a hybrid (keyword + vector) retrieval path.

days.hands: Add chat memory + robust error handling (retry on malformed output, typed fallback) to your service. This is the pattern your capstone will ship on.

exercise.summary: A demo becomes production-shaped when it remembers the conversation and survives failures. You'll add chat memory across turns, retries with a fallback model, and an output guard before returning. These three turn a happy-path demo into something you'd expose to users. Pitfall: unbounded memory — stuffing the entire history into every call quietly grows cost and latency until you hit the context limit; summarize or window it.
exercise.goal: Make the JVM LLM endpoint production-shaped.
steps: Add chat memory across turns.
Add retries + a fallback model on failure.
Validate/guard the output before returning.
acceptance: Multi-turn memory works within a session.
A forced provider failure falls back without erroring the caller.
exercise.stretch: Add a circuit breaker and surface it via a health endpoint.

weeks.udemy: Resources: Java Brains (on-ramp) + LangChain4j / Spring AI / Quarkus docs
weeks.deliverable: Repo billpay-ai-jvm — Quarkus/Kotlin service: tool-using, RAG-backed, typed, memory-aware LLM endpoint.

weeks.title: Foundation Model Internals
weeks.goal: Goal: understand why models behave as they do — the theory you skipped in Part I, now that you can contextualize it against systems you've built. Huyen Ch 2.

days.focusSub: temperature, top-k, top-p, greedy; structured outputs

resources.label: Mike X Cohen — Deep LLM Mechanisms

days.hands: Implement temperature + top-p sampling over raw logprobs; show how each reshapes the output distribution.

exercise.summary: Sampling parameters like temperature and top-p control how random a model's output is; this makes them concrete by experiment. You'll generate the same prompt at temperature 0, 0.7, and 1.2, sweep top-k/top-p, and find the setting that keeps a structured output valid. Now the knobs mean something instead of being magic numbers. Beginner trap: cranking temperature for "creativity" on a task that needs reliability — high randomness breaks JSON and reproducibility.
exercise.goal: Make sampling parameters concrete by experiment.
steps: Generate completions at temperature 0, 0.7, 1.2 on the same prompt.
Sweep top-k and top-p and observe diversity vs coherence.
Force a structured output and find the sampling that keeps it valid.
acceptance: A short writeup ties each parameter to an observed effect.
You can state the sampling you'd use for deterministic extraction.
exercise.stretch: Reproduce a 'temperature too high breaks JSON' failure and fix it.

days.focusSub: CoT, best-of-N, ToT, parallel/sequential sampling, search-against-verifier
days.hands: Implement best-of-N, self-consistency, and a simple Tree-of-Thoughts + verifier search on a SQL/math task; chart accuracy vs token cost.

exercise.summary: You can buy accuracy at inference time by having the model do more work — sampling several answers and picking the best, or searching over reasoning paths. You'll implement best-of-N with a simple scorer and a tiny Tree-of-Thoughts loop, then compare accuracy against one greedy call. The tradeoff is quality versus tokens and latency. Pitfall: assuming more samples always help — without a decent scorer to choose among them, best-of-N just adds cost.
exercise.goal: Buy quality with test-time compute.
steps: Implement best-of-N sampling with a simple scorer.
Implement a tiny Tree-of-Thoughts or search-against-verifier loop.
Compare accuracy vs a single greedy call on a small task set.
acceptance: best-of-N beats single-shot on your task set.
A verifier/score gates the final answer.
exercise.stretch: Plot accuracy vs N and find the cost/quality knee.

resources.label: Huyen Ch 2 — The Probabilistic Nature of AI

days.hands: Run the same prompt 20× at temp 0 vs 1; quantify variance. Design a determinism strategy for billpay-grade reliability.

exercise.summary: Models are probabilistic, so the same prompt can give different answers and sometimes confident wrong ones (hallucinations); here you quantify it. You'll run one prompt 10 times to measure variance, build a prompt that reliably hallucinates and one that doesn't, and note which mitigations (grounding, lower temperature) help. Seeing the variance first-hand changes how much you trust a single output. Beginner trap: treating one good run as proof — always check behaviour across repeated runs.
exercise.goal: Quantify the probabilistic nature + hallucination.
steps: Run the same prompt 10× and measure answer variance.
Construct a prompt that reliably hallucinates and one that doesn't.
Note which mitigations (grounding, lower temp) reduce it.
acceptance: Variance across runs is measured, not hand-waved.
A before/after shows a mitigation reducing hallucination.
exercise.stretch: Add self-consistency voting and measure the lift.

days.focusSub: SFT, RLHF, DPO; reward modeling (ORM/PRM), STaR, Meta-CoT
days.hands: Read a DPO preference dataset; diagram outcome- vs process-reward models (ORM/PRM) and how STaR bootstraps reasoning data. Sketch preference data for a billpay assistant.

exercise.summary: A base model becomes a helpful assistant through post-training; this traces that pipeline. You'll write notes distinguishing Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT), Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF), and Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), summarize outcome vs process reward models, and map where reasoning ability comes from. It's the vocabulary behind "why does this model behave this way". Pitfall: conflating SFT with RLHF — one teaches format from examples, the other shapes preference from comparisons.
exercise.goal: Trace the post-training pipeline that shapes behaviour.
steps: Write notes distinguishing SFT vs RLHF vs DPO.
Summarize reward modeling (ORM vs PRM) in your own words.
Map STaR / Meta-CoT to where reasoning ability comes from.
acceptance: A one-page note correctly separates the three post-training stages.
You can explain ORM vs PRM with an example.
exercise.stretch: Find a real model card and identify which stages it used.

days.focusSub: data corpora (Common Crawl/FineWeb), BPE, params, context, MoE
days.hands: Map the pre-training pipeline (corpora → cleaning → BPE → architecture). Build a decision table: per task class, which model size/family + why.

exercise.summary: This connects pre-training choices — data, tokenizer, architecture, size — to what a model can do. You'll summarize the big corpora and Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE)'s role, estimate the params-to-memory relationship for a given size, and explain Mixture-of-Experts versus dense models in three bullets. It demystifies why a 70B model needs the hardware it does. Beginner trap: equating parameter count with quality — data quality and training choices often matter more than raw size.
exercise.goal: Connect pre-training choices to model capability.
steps: Summarize data corpora (Common Crawl/FineWeb) and BPE's role.
Compute a rough params↔memory estimate for a given size.
Explain MoE vs dense in 3 bullets.
acceptance: A memory estimate for one model size is shown with the formula.
MoE tradeoffs are stated correctly.
exercise.stretch: Compare context-length scaling costs for two architectures.

days.focusSub: optional deep path — what's inside the weights

resources.label: Mike X Cohen — Deep LLM Mechanisms

days.hands: Optional: probe attention heads / activations on a small model. Pull the relevant module from Cohen's course — don't attempt all 90h; sample interpretability + attention internals.

exercise.summary: Mechanistic interpretability is the attempt to read what's actually happening inside a model's weights; this is one optional peek. You'll run a small notebook (a logit lens or an induction-head demo), identify one interpretable behaviour, and write three sentences on it. It's a humbling reminder these aren't total black boxes. Pitfall: over-reading a toy demo — a clean result on a tiny model rarely generalizes to a frontier one.
exercise.goal: Take one peek inside the weights (optional).
steps: Run a small mechanistic-interpretability notebook (e.g. logit lens / an induction head demo).
Identify one interpretable behaviour.
Write 3 sentences on what you saw.
acceptance: A notebook runs and surfaces one interpretable signal.
You can describe it without jargon.
exercise.stretch: Probe a second behaviour and compare.

weeks.udemy: Spine: Huyen Ch 2 + ByteByteGo P1/P4 · Cohen (optional)
weeks.deliverable: Repo week-05-fm-internals — sampling + ToT/verifier eval + reward-model notes + determinism writeup.

weeks.title: Context Engineering & RAG
weeks.goal: Goal: build RAG from scratch (no framework), then rebuild with a framework. Corpus: Temporal docs — eat your own dog food. (Deep retrieval theory comes back in Week 7.)

days.hands: Write a 1-pager: 3 chunking strategies + when each wins.

exercise.summary: Before retrieval you must split documents into chunks and turn them into embeddings (vectors that capture meaning); how you chunk silently decides quality. You'll chunk one corpus three ways (fixed, recursive, semantic), embed with all-MiniLM-L6-v2, and eyeball results on a few queries. Chunking is the cheapest, highest-leverage knob on a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipeline. Beginner trap: defaulting to fixed-size chunks that slice sentences in half — the model then retrieves fragments that read as nonsense.
exercise.goal: Choose chunking deliberately, not by default.
steps: Chunk one corpus 3 ways (fixed, recursive, semantic).
Embed each with all-MiniLM-L6-v2.
Eyeball retrieval quality on 3 queries per strategy.
acceptance: A 1-pager states 3 chunking strategies and when each wins.
You picked a default with a reason.
exercise.stretch: Add overlap and measure its effect on recall.

days.hands: Ingest Temporal docs; chunk 3 ways; embed with all-MiniLM-L6-v2.

exercise.summary: You'll build a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipeline end to end with no framework, so you understand every part before a library hides it. Ingest a docs corpus into Chroma, retrieve the top-k chunks, stuff them into the prompt, answer with the source chunks cited, and add a guard that says "not in context" when nothing relevant is found. Doing it raw demystifies every RAG product you'll meet. Beginner trap: skipping the "not in context" guard — without it the model happily fabricates an answer when retrieval finds nothing.
exercise.goal: Build RAG end-to-end with no framework.
steps: Ingest a docs corpus into Chroma using the raw anthropic + chromadb + sentence-transformers.
Retrieve top-k and stuff context into the prompt.
Return answers with source chunks.
acceptance: A question is answered using retrieved context.
Sources are shown alongside the answer.
exercise.stretch: Add a 'no answer in context' guard.

days.hands: Add a re-ranker (cohere/rerank free or bge-reranker-v2-m3). Compare with/without.

exercise.summary: Reranking improves the retrieve→augment→generate loop by re-scoring retrieved chunks so the best ones land in the prompt. You'll add a reranker (Cohere's free tier or bge-reranker), compare answers with and without it on five queries, and record where it changed the top result. Retrieval gets you candidates; reranking gets you the right one. Pitfall: reranking a huge candidate set — it's slower and costlier, so retrieve a sane top-N first, then rerank that.
exercise.goal: Add reranking to the retrieve→augment→generate loop.
steps: Add a reranker (cohere/rerank free tier or bge-reranker-v2-m3).
Compare answers with and without rerank on 5 queries.
Record where rerank changed the top result.
acceptance: A measurable quality difference with/without rerank is recorded.
The reranked pipeline is the new default.
exercise.stretch: Add MMR for diversity and compare.

days.hands: Rebuild in LlamaIndex/LangChain; implement hybrid + HyDE + query rewriting.

exercise.summary: Now you rebuild your scratch pipeline in a framework (LlamaIndex or LangChain) to see exactly what it automates — and what it hides. Add hybrid retrieval, HyDE (Hypothetical Document Embeddings), and query rewriting, then diff the framework version against your hand-built one. Frameworks are great once you know what they're doing for you. Beginner trap: adopting a framework before understanding the basics — when it breaks, you won't know which layer failed.
exercise.goal: Rebuild in a framework to see what it hides.
steps: Rebuild the pipeline in LlamaIndex or LangChain.
Add hybrid retrieval, HyDE, and query rewriting.
Diff the framework version against your scratch version.
acceptance: The framework version matches scratch behaviour.
HyDE + query rewriting are wired and tested.
exercise.stretch: Note which framework abstraction cost you control.

days.focusSub: pgvector swap · HNSW vs IVF · when to pick Qdrant/Weaviate/Pinecone

resources.label: Hamel — most RAG problems are retrieval problems
Qdrant benchmarks (vs pgvector / Weaviate / Pinecone / Milvus)

days.hands: Swap Chroma → pgvector; record recall@5 / MRR across chunking strategies in results.md. Then write a one-pager: HNSW vs IVF (when each wins on recall / latency / memory) + a decision matrix — Chroma / pgvector / Qdrant / Weaviate / Pinecone — scored on millions-of-vectors, metadata filtering, multi-tenancy, self-host vs SaaS.

exercise.summary: This makes RAG production-shaped and forces a real vector-database choice. You'll swap Chroma for pgvector, record recall@5 and Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR) across chunking strategies, write the HNSW-vs-IVF index tradeoff, and score five databases on a decision matrix. Picking a vector database is an architecture decision, not a default. Pitfall: choosing on benchmarks alone — operational fit (does it run next to your Postgres? metadata filtering?) usually matters more than raw speed.
exercise.goal: Make RAG production-shaped and pick a vector DB.
steps: Swap Chroma → pgvector; record recall@5 / MRR across chunking strategies in results.md.
Write the HNSW-vs-IVF tradeoff (recall/latency/memory).
Score Chroma/pgvector/Qdrant/Weaviate/Pinecone on a decision matrix.
acceptance: recall@5 / MRR numbers are recorded for pgvector.
A decision matrix justifies a vector-DB pick for millions of vectors + metadata filtering.
exercise.stretch: Load-test pgvector at 100k vectors and note p95 latency.

weeks.udemy: Udemy: Core Track — Week 6
weeks.deliverable: Repo week-06-rag — answers "what is sticky queue starvation?" with citations + numbers + vector-DB decision matrix.

weeks.title: Advanced RAG & Agents
weeks.goal: Goal: the retrieval and agent depth that actually moves production quality — beyond the basic pipeline you built in Week 6. Huyen Ch 6.

days.hands: Implement BM25 + dense + RRF fusion on your W6 corpus; compare recall across the three.

exercise.summary: This implements the core retrieval algorithms so you can compare them on your own data. You'll build BM25 (keyword ranking), dense (embedding) retrieval, and Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) to combine them, then compare recall and pick a winner with justification. Hybrid usually beats either alone. Beginner trap: assuming embeddings always win — keyword search still dominates for exact terms, codes, and rare names that embeddings smear together.
exercise.goal: Implement and compare the core retrieval algorithms.
steps: Implement BM25, dense, and RRF fusion over your W6 corpus.
Compare recall across the three.
Pick the winner for your data and justify it.
acceptance: recall is compared across BM25/dense/fusion.
A winner is chosen with numbers.
exercise.stretch: Add metadata pre-filtering and re-measure.

days.hands: Add a cross-encoder reranker + query rewriting; measure nDCG lift vs the W6 baseline.

exercise.summary: Here you push retrieval past the baseline with reranking, query rewriting, and contextual retrieval. You'll add a cross-encoder reranker and Anthropic-style contextual retrieval, then measure the ranking-quality (nDCG) lift against your earlier baseline. Always measure the lift — optimizations that feel better often don't move the metric. Pitfall: stacking techniques with no baseline number — you can't tell which one helped, or whether you made it worse.
exercise.goal: Optimise retrieval beyond the baseline.
steps: Add a cross-encoder reranker + query rewriting.
Add contextual retrieval (Anthropic-style).
Measure nDCG lift vs the W6 baseline.
acceptance: nDCG improves over baseline and the delta is recorded.
Each optimisation's individual contribution is noted.
exercise.stretch: Try contextual-retrieval prompt caching to cut cost.

days.hands: Add tabular retrieval over your Oracle schema — text-to-SQL plus row retrieval — feeding the agent.

exercise.summary: Real questions often need structured data, not just prose, so retrieval expands to tables and databases. You'll add text-to-SQL plus row retrieval over a real schema, feed retrieved rows to the agent, and test three questions that require structured data. Many "the AI got the number wrong" bugs are really retrieval-over-text bugs. Beginner trap: letting a model free-write SQL against production — constrain it, validate it, and run it read-only.
exercise.goal: Retrieve beyond plain text.
steps: Add tabular retrieval over your Oracle schema (text-to-SQL + row retrieval).
Feed retrieved rows into the agent.
Test 3 questions that require structured data.
acceptance: The agent answers a question needing SQL/tabular retrieval.
Wrong-table/empty-result paths are handled.
exercise.stretch: Add a multimodal (image/table) retrieval example.

days.hands: Add a memory layer to your capstone agent (vector recall + rolling summary); test multi-session recall.

exercise.summary: Agents feel stateless until you give them memory — recall of past turns and a rolling summary. You'll add vector recall plus a rolling summary to your agent, persist it across sessions, and test multi-session recall on a scripted conversation. Memory is what makes an assistant feel continuous. Pitfall: remembering everything — unbounded memory balloons cost and surfaces stale facts; decide what to keep, summarize, and forget.
exercise.goal: Give your agent real memory.
steps: Add a memory layer (vector recall + rolling summary) to the capstone agent.
Persist across sessions.
Test multi-session recall on a scripted conversation.
acceptance: The agent recalls facts from a prior session.
Summary memory keeps context within the window.
exercise.stretch: Add episodic memory and test retrieval of a specific past event.

resources.label: Huyen Ch 6 — Agent Failure Modes and Evaluation

days.hands: Build a failure taxonomy + targeted evals for your agent; add loop/cost circuit-breakers.

exercise.summary: Agents fail in characteristic ways — bad plans, tool errors, infinite loops — and naming them is the first step to catching them. You'll catalogue the failures you actually observe, write a targeted eval for each, and add loop and cost circuit-breakers. A failure taxonomy turns "it's flaky" into a checklist. Beginner trap: no circuit-breaker — one looping agent can burn a surprising amount of money before you notice.
exercise.goal: Build a failure taxonomy + targeted evals for the agent.
steps: Catalogue planning errors, tool errors, and loops you observe.
Write targeted evals for each failure mode.
Add loop and cost circuit-breakers.
acceptance: A failure taxonomy with example traces exists.
Circuit-breakers stop runaway loops/cost.
exercise.stretch: Wire the evals into CI as a gate.

days.hands: Implement Reflexion (self-critique memory) and ReWOO (plan-then-execute) variants of your agent; compare against plain ReAct on your eval set.

exercise.summary: This implements smarter agent loops beyond plain ReAct: Reflexion (the agent critiques and retries) and ReWOO (Reasoning WithOut Observation — plan first, then execute). You'll run both against your eval set and compare quality and cost to a ReAct baseline. Fancier loops aren't automatically better. Pitfall: adding reflection everywhere — the extra calls can double cost for a quality gain that only shows up on hard tasks.
exercise.goal: Implement advanced agent reasoning variants.
steps: Implement Reflexion (self-critique memory) and ReWOO (plan-then-execute).
Run both against your eval set.
Compare to plain ReAct on quality and cost.
acceptance: Reflexion and ReWOO both run on your task.
A comparison table vs ReAct is recorded.
exercise.stretch: Add Tree-of-Thoughts and compare on a planning task.

weeks.udemy: Spine: Huyen Ch 6
weeks.deliverable: Repo week-07-advanced-rag — hybrid+rerank + tabular RAG + agent memory + failure evals.

weeks.title: Agents — Build Without Frameworks
weeks.goal: Goal: write a ReAct loop in ~100 lines. Once you grok this, every framework becomes a thin wrapper in your mind.

resources.label: Anthropic — How We Build Effective Agents

days.hands: Diagram the ReAct loop on paper before writing any code.

exercise.summary: Before building agents you need a crisp definition separating a true agent from a scripted workflow. You'll write your own definition, list the components (model, tools, loop, memory, stop condition), and sketch the smallest agent for your domain. A clear mental model prevents over-engineering. Beginner trap: calling every model call an "agent" — if there's no loop and no tool use it's a workflow, and a workflow is often the better choice.
exercise.goal: Define precisely what an agent is (and isn't).
steps: Write your own definition separating workflows from agents.
List the components: model, tools, loop, memory, stop condition.
Sketch the smallest agent for your domain.
acceptance: A crisp written definition with the component list.
A diagram of your minimal agent.
exercise.stretch: Map each component to a Temporal primitive.

days.focusSub: The most important exercise in the course
days.hands: Write the loop yourself with 5 tools: web search (Tavily free), file r/w, python REPL, calculator, current_date.

exercise.summary: You'll write a ReAct agent — reason, act, observe — in about 100 lines with no framework. Wire it to your earlier RAG plus a calculator tool, log every thought/action/observation, and add a max-step stop. Seeing the raw loop makes every agent framework legible afterward. Pitfall: no step cap — a confused ReAct agent loops until it runs out of money; the stop condition is not optional.
exercise.goal: Write a ReAct agent in ~100 lines, no framework.
steps: Implement reason→act→observe with your W6 RAG + a calculator tool.
Log every step (thought, action, observation).
Add a max-step stop condition.
acceptance: The agent solves a multi-step task using tools.
Every step is logged and the loop terminates safely.
exercise.stretch: Add a retry on malformed tool calls.

days.hands: Sketch each Anthropic pattern as a flow.

exercise.summary: This catalogues the reusable agent patterns — chaining, routing, parallelization, orchestrator-workers, evaluator-optimizer — with a when-to-use for each. You'll map each to a real task and pick the one your capstone needs. Most "agent" problems are really one of these patterns in disguise. Beginner trap: reaching for a complex multi-agent setup when simple prompt chaining would do — match the pattern to the task, not the hype.
exercise.goal: Catalogue the agentic patterns with examples.
steps: Write a one-liner + when-to-use for chaining, routing, parallelization, orchestrator-workers, evaluator-optimizer.
Map each to a real task you have.
Pick the pattern your capstone needs.
acceptance: All 5 patterns have a use-case mapped.
Your capstone's primary pattern is chosen.
exercise.stretch: Note the failure mode of each pattern.

days.hands: Each pattern as a standalone example. Note: when would you use each inside a Temporal workflow?

exercise.summary: Now you actually implement those five patterns and compare them on the same task. Build chaining, routing, and parallelization, then orchestrator-workers and evaluator-optimizer. Implementing them cements when each pays off. Pitfall: evaluator-optimizer loops with no exit criterion — define "good enough" or they iterate forever.
exercise.goal: Implement the 5 agentic patterns.
steps: Build chaining, routing, and parallelization.
Build orchestrator-workers and evaluator-optimizer.
Compare on the same task where sensible.
acceptance: All 5 patterns run on real input.
A short note says which fit your workflow.
exercise.stretch: Compose two patterns (e.g. router → workers) into one flow.

weeks.udemy: Udemy: Agentic Track (foundations) + Core Track Wk 8
weeks.deliverable: Repo week-08-agents-from-scratch — ReAct + 5 patterns + Temporal-context comparison.

weeks.title: Multi-Framework Agents + MCP
weeks.goal: Goal: become fluent across the four frameworks the Agentic Track teaches — build the same agent in each, then form opinions on each vs a real workflow engine. Breadth here makes you fluent in any team's stack.

days.focusSub: state, nodes, edges, checkpointer, HITL ≈ Temporal
days.hands: Port your W8 ReAct agent to LangGraph; add a checkpointer + human-in-the-loop interrupt. Note where this mirrors Temporal signals.

exercise.summary: LangGraph models an agent as an explicit state graph — nodes, edges, and a checkpointer for resumability. You'll build the reference agent in it, add a human-in-the-loop interrupt, and map each concept to Temporal in notes. Graphs make complex control flow inspectable. Beginner trap: hiding real branching logic inside one giant node — the graph only helps if the steps are actually separate nodes.
exercise.goal: Build the reference agent in LangGraph.
steps: Model state, nodes, edges, and a checkpointer.
Add a human-in-the-loop interrupt.
Map LangGraph concepts to Temporal in notes.
acceptance: The agent runs as a graph with a checkpointer.
A HITL interrupt pauses and resumes correctly.
exercise.stretch: Add a conditional edge that routes on tool output.

days.hands: Rebuild the same agent in the OpenAI Agents SDK; compare its handoff & guardrail primitives to LangGraph edges.

exercise.summary: This rebuilds the same agent on the OpenAI Agents SDK to feel a different framework's ergonomics. You'll implement handoffs between two agents, add guardrails and a session, and compare the developer experience to LangGraph. Building the same thing twice is the fastest way to judge a framework. Pitfall: agent-to-agent handoffs with no guardrail — one agent can hand off a bad instruction the next executes blindly.
exercise.goal: Rebuild it on the OpenAI Agents SDK.
steps: Implement handoffs between two agents.
Add guardrails and a session.
Compare ergonomics to LangGraph.
acceptance: A handoff passes control with context intact.
A guardrail blocks a disallowed input.
exercise.stretch: Add tracing and inspect a run.

days.hands: A 3-role crew (researcher → analyst → writer) producing a billpay incident summary.

exercise.summary: CrewAI frames a multi-agent system as a crew of roles, each with a goal and tools. You'll define 2–3 roles, run the crew on the shared task, and note where the role abstraction helped versus added overhead. Role framing is intuitive but isn't free. Beginner trap: inventing roles that don't map to real subtasks — extra agents add coordination cost without adding capability.
exercise.goal: Rebuild it as a CrewAI role-based crew.
steps: Define 2–3 roles with goals and tools.
Run the crew on the shared task.
Note where role abstraction helped/hurt.
acceptance: The crew completes the task collaboratively.
A short comparison vs the prior two frameworks.
exercise.stretch: Add a manager/critic role and measure quality.

days.hands: A group-chat of agents solving a task; note how orchestration/turn-taking differs from CrewAI roles.

exercise.summary: AutoGen models agents as a conversational group chat that talk to each other to solve a task. You'll set up the group chat, run the shared task, and capture how termination and cost behave. It's a very different mental model from a fixed graph. Pitfall: no termination condition — group chats can ramble turn after turn, quietly running up token cost.
exercise.goal: Rebuild it with AutoGen group chat.
steps: Set up a conversational multi-agent group chat.
Run the shared task.
Capture termination and cost behaviour.
acceptance: The group chat reaches a correct result and terminates.
Cost vs the other frameworks is noted.
exercise.stretch: Add a tool-using agent to the group.

days.focusSub: build your demo server, then judge them all
days.hands: Build: an MCP server exposing your Oracle DB read-only (reused later). Write "4 frameworks vs Temporal as a workflow engine" — a portfolio piece only you can write.

exercise.summary: The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open standard for exposing tools and data to any model over a common interface — build a server once, call it from anywhere. You'll build a small MCP server for one of your tools, call it from an agent via an MCP client, and write the verdict comparing all four agent frameworks. MCP is becoming the universal connector for tool integration. Beginner trap: exposing a write-capable tool over MCP with no auth or guardrail — treat an MCP server like any public API surface.
exercise.goal: Build an MCP server and deliver the verdict.
steps: Build a small MCP server exposing one of your tools.
Call it from an agent via an MCP client.
Write the comparison doc across all four frameworks + when to use each.
acceptance: The same agent runs against your MCP tool.
A comparison doc picks a default framework with reasons.
exercise.stretch: Publish the MCP server and consume it from two different clients.

weeks.udemy: Udemy: Agentic Track (full course) — OpenAI SDK · CrewAI · LangGraph · AutoGen · MCP
weeks.deliverable: Repo week-09-frameworks — same agent ×4 + Oracle MCP server + comparison doc.

weeks.title: Temporal + LLM Orchestration
weeks.goal: Goal — YOUR DIFFERENTIATOR. No course teaches this. Run agents as durable, throttled, observable Temporal workflows at 500K+ scale. This is why you're hireable; the capstone is built on it.

resources.label: Temporal — Why Every AI Agent Needs Durable Execution

days.hands: Wrap each LLM call as an Activity; keep non-determinism out of Workflow code. Port your W9 agent into a Temporal workflow skeleton.

exercise.summary: Durable workflow engines like Temporal make flaky agents reliable by recording every step so a crash can resume exactly where it left off. You'll wrap each model/tool call as a Temporal Activity, keep non-determinism out of workflow code, and port an earlier agent into a workflow skeleton. This is how agents survive real-world failures. Pitfall: putting a random or time-dependent call directly in workflow code — non-determinism breaks Temporal's replay; it belongs in an Activity.
exercise.goal: Map an agent onto a durable workflow.
steps: Wrap each LLM/tool call as a Temporal Activity.
Keep all non-determinism out of Workflow code.
Port your W9 agent into a Temporal workflow skeleton.
acceptance: The agent runs as a workflow with activities.
Workflow code is deterministic (no direct I/O).
exercise.stretch: Add a replay test proving determinism.

resources.label: Temporal — Why Every AI Agent Needs Durable Execution

days.hands: Add backoff retries + timeouts to LLM/tool Activities; make tool side-effects idempotent.

exercise.summary: Model and tool calls fail intermittently, so you add the reliability primitives that absorb it. You'll add backoff retries and timeouts to activities, heartbeating for long calls, and make one side-effecting tool idempotent. Idempotency is what makes a retry safe. Beginner trap: retrying a non-idempotent action like "send payment" — a blind retry can do it twice.
exercise.goal: Make flaky LLM calls reliable.
steps: Add backoff retries + timeouts to LLM/tool activities.
Add activity heartbeating for long calls.
Make a tool side-effect idempotent.
acceptance: A transient failure is retried transparently.
Re-running an idempotent tool causes no double effect.
exercise.stretch: Inject a 30% failure rate and confirm completion.

resources.label: Temporal — Why Every AI Agent Needs Durable Execution

days.hands: Add an approval gate via Signal; expose live agent state via Query; executeUpdateWithStart for kick-off + first input.

exercise.summary: Some actions need a human's yes before they run; durable Signals and Queries make that gate first-class. You'll add an approval gate via a Signal, expose live agent state via a Query, and start the workflow with its first input atomically. This is how you ship agents that take real actions safely. Pitfall: blocking forever waiting for approval — add a timeout and a default so a forgotten request doesn't wedge the workflow.
exercise.goal: Add human-in-the-loop via Signals/Queries.
steps: Add an approval gate using a Signal.
Expose live agent state via a Query.
Use executeUpdateWithStart for kickoff + first input.
acceptance: A workflow pauses for approval and resumes on Signal.
Current state is observable via Query.
exercise.stretch: Add a timeout that auto-rejects after N hours.

resources.label: Temporal Schedules · task-queue partitioning · worker tuning

days.hands: Trigger 500K workflows throttled — rate-limit LLM calls, cap concurrency, protect the r8g.8xlarge Postgres without scaling it. Schedules vs Workflow.sleep() at scale.

exercise.summary: Triggering thousands of agent runs at once will melt your model API and your database unless you throttle. You'll fire a large batch while rate-limiting model calls and capping concurrency, protect a fixed-size Postgres without scaling it, and compare scheduling approaches. Backpressure is a first-class design concern at scale. Beginner trap: unbounded fan-out — launching 500K workflows with no concurrency cap takes down the very dependencies they share.
exercise.goal: Throttle fan-out at 500K scale.
steps: Trigger a large batch of workflows, rate-limiting LLM calls and capping concurrency.
Protect the r8g.8xlarge Postgres without scaling it.
Compare Schedules vs Workflow.sleep() at scale.
acceptance: A large fan-out runs within concurrency/rate caps.
Postgres stays healthy under the load.
exercise.stretch: Chart throughput vs concurrency cap and find the safe ceiling.

resources.label: Blog: "What Agent Frameworks Get Wrong About Workflow Orchestration"

days.hands: Draft the post — it basically writes itself with your background. Your standout interview asset.

exercise.summary: Turning your build into a written argument is how it becomes interview and portfolio currency. You'll draft a blog post ("What Agent Frameworks Get Wrong About Workflow Orchestration"), back every claim with something from your own build, and get it review-ready. Specific, build-backed writing is far more credible than opinion. Pitfall: generic hot-takes with no evidence — anchor each claim to a concrete thing you measured or shipped.
exercise.goal: Write the orchestration thesis.
steps: Draft the blog: 'What Agent Frameworks Get Wrong About Workflow Orchestration'.
Back each claim with something from your build.
Get it review-ready.
acceptance: A complete draft with concrete examples from your code.
It states your differentiated POV clearly.
exercise.stretch: Publish and cross-post; capture one piece of feedback.

weeks.udemy: Udemy: none exists — this is uniquely yours.
weeks.deliverable: Repo week-10-temporal-llm — durable multi-agent workflow w/ HITL signals + throttled fan-out + blog draft.

weeks.title: Fine-Tuning & Local Models
weeks.goal: Goal: know when fine-tuning helps (rarely) and how to do it when it does. Decision tree: prompt → few-shot → RAG → fine-tune. (The memory math & quantization theory deepen in Week 12.)

resources.label: Hamel — Fine-Tuning is Probably Not For You

days.hands: Write your escalation decision tree; mark where billpay tasks would land.

exercise.summary: Fine-tuning is often the wrong first move; this is the decision exercise that proves whether you even need it. You'll walk the ladder — prompt → few-shot → RAG → fine-tune — for your task, write the case for and against, and define the metric that would justify the cost. Most "we need to fine-tune" instincts are solved cheaper upstream. Beginner trap: fine-tuning to add knowledge — for fresh facts RAG is usually better; fine-tuning is for behaviour and format.
exercise.goal: Decide whether fine-tuning is even justified.
steps: Walk the decision tree: prompt → few-shot → RAG → fine-tune for your task.
Write the case for/against fine-tuning here.
Define the metric that would prove it was worth it.
acceptance: A written decision with the metric that would justify FT.
You can argue why prompting/RAG is or isn't enough.
exercise.stretch: Estimate the data volume you'd need.

days.hands: Fine-tune Qwen2.5-3B with LoRA via unsloth on a free Colab T4. Task: SQL gen on a synthetic billpay schema.

exercise.summary: Full fine-tuning is expensive; Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) methods like LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) train small adapters instead, and QLoRA does it on a quantized base so it fits one GPU. You'll LoRA-tune a small model on a focused dataset, hold out a test set, and prove the tuned model beats base on it. Always measure against base — sometimes it doesn't win. Pitfall: reporting training loss as success — only a held-out test set shows whether you improved or just memorized.
exercise.goal: Run a LoRA/QLoRA fine-tune that beats base.
steps: Fine-tune a small model (e.g. Llama-3.2-3B) with LoRA/PEFT on a focused dataset.
Hold out a test set.
Compare tuned vs base on that set.
acceptance: The tuned model beats base on the held-out metric.
Training config + results are committed.
exercise.stretch: Try QLoRA (4-bit) and compare quality/VRAM.

days.hands: Serve your fine-tuned model with vLLM; benchmark tokens/sec vs Ollama.

exercise.summary: Running models locally matters for cost, privacy, and latency; this serves one two ways and previews quantization. You'll run the model with Ollama, then vLLM, quantize it, and re-measure quality and latency. Each server suits a different job — quick local dev versus high-throughput serving. Beginner trap: judging a server on a single request — the differences show up under concurrent load, not one-off calls.
exercise.goal: Serve the model locally and preview quantization.
steps: Run the model with Ollama, then with vLLM.
Quantize it and re-measure quality + latency.
Note when you'd choose each server.
acceptance: The model serves under both runtimes.
A quality/latency comparison for a quantized variant exists.
exercise.stretch: Expose an OpenAI-compatible endpoint and point your W3 script at it.

days.hands: Pre-train a tiny GPT on Tiny Shakespeare. Optional but you'll never see transformers the same way.

exercise.summary: This optional deep-dive reads a transformer from scratch — attention, blocks — so the architecture stops being a black box. You'll work a from-scratch notebook, re-derive one component in your own words, and connect it to a knob you tuned earlier. Depth here pays off when you debug weird model behaviour. Pitfall: getting lost in math for its own sake — aim to connect each piece to something observable, not to memorize equations.
exercise.goal: Read the transformer from the metal (optional).
steps: Work a from-scratch transformer notebook (attention, blocks).
Re-derive one component in your own words.
Connect it to a knob you tuned in W5.
acceptance: A notebook runs and you can explain attention end-to-end.
One component is re-derived in notes.
exercise.stretch: Implement a single attention head from scratch.

weeks.udemy: Udemy: Core Track — Week 8
weeks.deliverable: Repo week-11-finetuning — LoRA model beating base on held-out test + "when would I fine-tune at work?" writeup.

weeks.title: Finetuning Depth + Dataset Engineering
weeks.goal: Goal: the quantitative grounding behind fine-tuning, and the data work that actually decides whether it works — the half of fine-tuning nobody shows you. Huyen Ch 7–8.

resources.label: Mike X Cohen — Deep LLM Mechanisms

days.hands: Compute VRAM for full vs LoRA vs QLoRA on 7B/13B; verify against your actual Week-7 run.

exercise.summary: Whether a model fits your hardware is arithmetic, not guesswork. You'll write the params × bytes-per-dtype footprint formula, compute memory for FP32/FP16/BF16/INT8/INT4 for one model, and state what fits on your target GPU. This math decides every serving and training choice. Beginner trap: forgetting that training needs several times more memory than inference (gradients, optimizer states) — a model that serves fine may not train on the same card.
exercise.goal: Master the memory math behind training/serving.
steps: Write the footprint formula for params × bytes/dtype.
Compute memory for FP32/FP16/BF16/INT8/INT4 for one model.
State what fits on your target GPU.
acceptance: A memory sheet covers all 5 dtypes for one model.
You can say what fits in X GB.
exercise.stretch: Add KV-cache memory to the estimate for a given context length.

days.hands: Quantize your W11 model to Q4/Q5; benchmark quality + tokens/sec at each level.

exercise.summary: Quantization shrinks a model by storing weights in fewer bits, trading a little quality for big memory and speed wins. You'll quantize with GPTQ/AWQ and to GGUF (a common local-inference format), then measure quality and latency against full precision and plot the curve. Knowing the curve lets you pick the cheapest precision that's still good enough. Pitfall: quantizing too aggressively (e.g. 3-bit) and shipping without an eval — quality can fall off a cliff at the low end.
exercise.goal: Quantize and measure the tradeoff.
steps: Quantize a model with GPTQ/AWQ and to GGUF.
Measure quality + latency vs full precision.
Record the quality/latency curve.
acceptance: At least two quantization methods are benchmarked.
The quality/latency tradeoff is recorded.
exercise.stretch: Find the lowest bit-width that holds your quality bar.

days.hands: Merge two LoRA adapters with mergekit; eval the merged model on both source tasks.

exercise.summary: Model merging combines two fine-tuned models into one that can do both tasks, with no extra training. You'll merge two adapters with SLERP or TIES/DARE, evaluate on both source tasks, and check for capability regression. It's a cheap way to get multi-task ability. Beginner trap: assuming a merge keeps both skills intact — always re-test both tasks, because merging can quietly degrade one.
exercise.goal: Merge models for multi-task capability.
steps: Merge two adapters/models with SLERP or TIES/DARE.
Evaluate on both source tasks.
Check for capability regression.
acceptance: A merged model retains both tasks' ability.
Any regression is quantified.
exercise.stretch: Compare merge methods on the same pair.

days.hands: Build a 200-example billpay instruction dataset with an explicit quality rubric + coverage check.

exercise.summary: For fine-tuning, dataset quality decides success more than any hyperparameter. You'll assemble a small dataset with explicit quality/coverage/quantity targets, de-duplicate and clean it, and document your acquisition and annotation choices. A few hundred clean examples beat thousands of noisy ones. Pitfall: scraping volume and skipping cleaning — duplicates and label noise teach the model the wrong thing.
exercise.goal: Curate a dataset that actually decides FT success.
steps: Assemble a small dataset with explicit quality/coverage/quantity targets.
De-duplicate and clean.
Document acquisition + annotation choices.
acceptance: A cleaned, deduped dataset with a documented spec.
Coverage gaps are noted.
exercise.stretch: Add an inter-annotator agreement check on a sample.

days.hands: Synthesize + distill from a frontier model into your small model; dedup & filter; try RAFT (train the model to ignore distractor docs) and re-run the W11 eval to show lift.

exercise.summary: When you lack data, you can generate it — synthesis with a stronger model, distillation into a smaller one, and RAFT (Retrieval-Augmented Fine-Tuning) to teach use of retrieved context. You'll synthesize examples, distill into your small model, and apply RAFT. Synthetic data is powerful but needs guarding. Beginner trap: training on unfiltered synthetic data — errors from the generator get baked in and amplified.
exercise.goal: Generate data via synthesis, distillation, and RAFT.
steps: Synthesize training examples with a stronger model.
Distill into your small model.
Apply retrieval-augmented fine-tuning (RAFT).
acceptance: A synthetic set is generated and quality-filtered.
The distilled/RAFT model is evaluated against baseline.
exercise.stretch: Measure how much synthetic data closes the gap to the teacher.

weeks.udemy: Spine: Huyen Ch 7–8
weeks.deliverable: Repo week-12-data — memory-math sheet + quantized models + merged adapter + synthetic dataset.

weeks.title: Evals, Observability & Production Basics
weeks.goal: Goal: the most undervalued skill in AI engineering. After this week you're in the top 10% purely because most people skip evals. (The measurement theory — perplexity, judges, ranking — goes deep in Week 15.)

days.hands: List the failure modes you expect in your Week 9 agent.

exercise.summary: Evaluations (evals) are systematic tests of model quality, and they're the real differentiator between a demo and a product. You'll list the failure modes you expect from your agent, group them into categories, and pick the three that matter most. Knowing what to measure comes before building any grader. Beginner trap: "it looked good in a few tries" — vibes aren't evals; without measurement you can't tell if a change helped or hurt.
exercise.goal: Internalise why evals are the differentiator.
steps: List the failure modes you expect in your W9 agent.
Group them into categories.
Pick the 3 that matter most for your task.
acceptance: A categorised failure-mode list exists.
The top-3 are prioritised with reasons.
exercise.stretch: Find a real production incident write-up and map it to a failure mode.

days.hands: On your W9 agent: label 100 traces → cluster failures → LLM-as-judge per mode → validate (TPR/TNR) → golden set of 20–30 that gate CI.

exercise.summary: This builds a real eval suite the Hamel Husain way: look at your data, cluster failures, and write LLM-as-judge graders. You'll label 100 traces, write a judge per failure mode, validate each judge against your labels (its true-positive and true-negative rates), and keep a 20–30 item golden set that gates continuous integration (CI). The golden set is your regression alarm. Pitfall: trusting an unvalidated judge — a grader that disagrees with you is worse than no grader.
exercise.goal: Build an eval suite the Hamel way.
steps: Label 100 traces from your W9 agent.
Cluster failures and write an LLM-as-judge per mode.
Validate each judge (TPR/TNR) and keep a 20–30 golden set that gates CI.
acceptance: Judges are validated with TPR/TNR numbers.
A golden set gates CI.
exercise.stretch: Add a cost-per-eval-run budget to CI.

days.hands: Instrument every agent call (cost, latency, tools). Dashboard: "which tool calls fail most?"

exercise.summary: Observability means tracing every model and tool call — cost, latency, inputs, outputs — so you can debug what actually happened. You'll wire Langfuse into every agent call, build a dashboard answering "which tool calls fail most?", and trace one real failing session end to end. You can't fix what you can't see. Beginner trap: logging only final answers — without per-step traces, a multi-step failure is impossible to localize.
exercise.goal: Instrument everything with observability.
steps: Wire Langfuse into every agent call (cost, latency, tools).
Build a dashboard answering 'which tool calls fail most?'.
Trace one real failing session end-to-end.
acceptance: Every call is traced with cost+latency.
The dashboard answers the tool-failure question.
exercise.stretch: Add alerting on a cost or error-rate threshold.

days.hands: Add I/O guardrails. Try 10 attacks; document mitigations. (Expanded in Week 19.)

exercise.summary: Model apps have a new attack surface — prompt injection and jailbreaks — so you red-team yours and add guardrails. You'll add input and output guardrails, run 10 injection/jailbreak attacks (try Lakera Gandalf), and document each attack and its mitigation. Assume any text the model reads may be adversarial. Pitfall: guarding the input but not the output — a model can be tricked into emitting secrets or unsafe content that an output filter would catch.
exercise.goal: Red-team and add guardrails.
steps: Add input + output guardrails to the agent.
Run 10 prompt-injection / jailbreak attacks (try Lakera Gandalf).
Document each attack and its mitigation.
acceptance: Guardrails block the majority of attacks.
A red-team doc lists attacks + mitigations.
exercise.stretch: Add a canary-token test for data exfiltration.

weeks.udemy: Udemy: weak fit. Use the practitioners above; Maven AI Evals if you later invest.
weeks.deliverable: Repo week-13-evals — eval suite + Langfuse dashboard + 3 judges w/ TPR/TNR + red-team doc.

weeks.title: MLOps & Experiment Tracking
weeks.goal: Goal: add the ML lifecycle layer that observability (W13) doesn't cover — prompt / dataset / model versioning, experiment tracking, model registry. Once this is wired, every later result you ship is reproducible and every model upgrade can be gated. This is what makes "AI Engineer" sit next to "MLOps" on a job spec.

days.focusSub: MLflow + Weights & Biases — the runs/params/metrics primitive
days.hands: Re-run your W11 LoRA fine-tune with MLflow tracking: log hyperparams, train/eval loss curves, the LoRA adapter as an artifact, the eval-set metrics. Add a second run with a different learning rate; compare runs in the UI.

exercise.summary: Experiment tracking makes machine-learning work reproducible — params, metrics, and artifacts logged per run instead of lost in your terminal. You'll re-run an earlier LoRA fine-tune under MLflow tracking, add a second run with a different learning rate, and compare them in the UI. Without tracking, "which run was that?" has no answer. Beginner trap: not logging the exact data and config — a metric you can't reproduce is just an anecdote.
exercise.goal: Make experiments reproducible with tracking.
steps: Re-run your W11 LoRA fine-tune under MLflow tracking (params, loss curves, the adapter artifact, eval metrics).
Add a second run with a different learning rate.
Compare runs in the UI.
acceptance: Two runs are logged and comparable in MLflow.
The adapter is stored as an artifact.
exercise.stretch: Add W&B and log the same run to both.

resources.label: Hamel — A field guide to prompt management

days.hands: Move your capstone's 3 hottest prompts into Langfuse with versions. In code, fetch by name+label (e.g. billpay-classifier:production). Run a quick A/B between v1 and v2; record judge scores on each.

exercise.summary: Prompts are production code and deserve versioning, not edits buried in source. You'll move your three hottest prompts into Langfuse with versions, fetch them by name and label in code (e.g. billpay-classifier:production), and A/B v1 vs v2 with judge scores. Versioned prompts let you roll back a bad change instantly. Pitfall: editing the live prompt in place — with no version history you can't diff or revert when quality drops.
exercise.goal: Version prompts like software.
steps: Move your capstone's 3 hottest prompts into Langfuse with versions.
Fetch by name+label in code (e.g. billpay-classifier:production).
A/B v1 vs v2 and record judge scores.
acceptance: Prompts are fetched by version at runtime.
An A/B result picks a winner with numbers.
exercise.stretch: Wire a rollback to the previous prompt version.

days.focusSub: MLflow Registry / HF Hub — "which model is in prod?"
days.hands: Push the W11 LoRA adapter to HF Hub with a model card (training data, eval scores, intended use, known failure modes). Register in MLflow with stages staging → production. Document the promotion criteria.

exercise.summary: A model registry tracks which model version is where, with a model card documenting data, eval scores, and intended use. You'll push an adapter to the Hugging Face Hub with a model card, register it in MLflow with staging→production stages, and document promotion criteria. Lineage answers "what's in production and why". Beginner trap: promoting to production with no written criteria — "it seemed better" isn't a gate you can audit later.
exercise.goal: Stand up a model registry + lineage.
steps: Push the W11 adapter to HF Hub with a model card (data, eval scores, intended use, failure modes).
Register it in MLflow with staging→production stages.
Document the promotion criteria.
acceptance: A model card + registry entry exist with stages.
Promotion criteria are written down.
exercise.stretch: Script a promotion that checks the eval gate first.

days.focusSub: DVC / HF Datasets — eval sets and training data have versions too

resources.label: Eugene Yan — Monitoring ML in Production
