days.hands: Version your W13 golden eval set with DVC; tag eval-v1 after CI passes. Sketch a drift detector — embedding-distance histogram between training distribution and a week of production traces.

exercise.summary: Datasets change over time, so you version them and watch for drift — when production data stops resembling what you evaluated on. You'll version your golden eval set with DVC (Data Version Control), sketch an embedding-distance drift detector (train vs a week of prod), and pick a drift threshold. Drift is how a once-good system silently degrades. Pitfall: versioning code but not data — an eval result is meaningless if you can't pin the exact dataset that produced it.
exercise.goal: Version datasets and watch for drift.
steps: Version your W13 golden eval set with DVC; tag eval-v1.
Sketch a drift detector (embedding-distance histogram: train vs a week of prod traces).
Decide a drift threshold.
acceptance: The eval set is DVC-versioned and tagged.
A drift metric + threshold are defined.
exercise.stretch: Simulate drift and confirm the detector fires.

days.focusSub: GitHub Actions + your W13 evals as the gate

resources.label: Eugene Yan — Continuous Integration for ML

days.hands: GitHub Actions workflow on PR: run your W13 eval suite against the proposed prompt/model version, fail PR if any judge regresses below threshold. Wire MLflow run URL into the PR comment. This is the foundation W19 generalises to full deploy gating.

exercise.summary: This puts evals in the pipeline: a prompt or model change can't merge unless it passes. You'll add a GitHub Actions job that runs your evals on a PR's version, fails the PR if any judge regresses below threshold, and posts the run URL into the PR comment. The gate makes quality non-negotiable. Beginner trap: a gate with no teeth — if a failing eval only warns instead of blocking, regressions ship anyway.
exercise.goal: Gate deploys on evals in CI.
steps: Add a GitHub Actions job that runs the W13 evals on a PR's prompt/model version.
Fail the PR if any judge regresses below threshold.
Post the MLflow run URL into the PR comment.
acceptance: The CI job blocks a regressing PR.
The run URL appears on the PR.
exercise.stretch: Add a manual-approval step for production promotion.

weeks.udemy: Udemy: weak fit — use docs above + W&B Courses (free) for depth.
weeks.deliverable: Repo week-14-mlops — MLflow runs + Langfuse prompt versions + HF Hub model + DVC-versioned eval set + eval-gated CI workflow.

weeks.title: Evaluation, Deep
weeks.goal: Goal: the measurement foundations behind Week 13. This is what separates engineers who think their system works from those who can prove it. Huyen Ch 3–4.

resources.label: Mike X Cohen — Deep LLM Mechanisms

days.hands: Compute perplexity of a local model on a held-out billpay-docs set; interpret what it tells you.

exercise.summary: This computes the classic language-modeling metrics by hand so they stop being jargon. You'll compute cross-entropy and perplexity on a held-out set, relate bits-per-character to perplexity, and interpret what a perplexity delta actually means. Knowing the math grounds every benchmark you'll read. Pitfall: comparing perplexity across different tokenizers — the numbers aren't comparable unless the tokenization matches.
exercise.goal: Compute language-modeling metrics by hand.
steps: Compute cross-entropy and perplexity on a held-out set.
Relate BPC/BPB to perplexity.
Interpret what a perplexity delta means.
acceptance: Perplexity is computed correctly on real text.
You can explain what a change in it implies.
exercise.stretch: Compare perplexity of two models on the same set.

resources.label: Huyen Ch 3 — AI as a Judge

days.hands: Build an LLM judge with an explicit rubric; validate against human labels (TPR/TNR); measure position/verbosity bias.

exercise.summary: Using a model to grade outputs (LLM-as-judge) scales evaluation, but only if you validate the judge and watch its biases. You'll build a judge for one criterion with a rubric, validate it against human labels (true-positive/true-negative rate), and probe for position, verbosity, and self-preference bias. A judge you haven't checked can be confidently wrong. Beginner trap: trusting judge scores without human spot-checks — judges favor longer, more confident answers regardless of correctness.
exercise.goal: Use LLM-as-judge with eyes open.
steps: Build a judge for one criterion with a rubric.
Validate it against human labels (TPR/TNR).
Probe for position/verbosity/self bias.
acceptance: The judge is validated against humans.
At least one bias is demonstrated and mitigated.
exercise.stretch: Add pairwise judging and compare to pointwise.

days.hands: Pairwise-compare 3 models on your task; compute an Elo ranking from the results.

exercise.summary: Sometimes you can't score answers absolutely, but you can compare them pairwise and rank. You'll run pairwise comparisons on a task set, compute Elo to build a mini arena, and produce a ranked scorecard. Relative ranking is often more reliable than absolute scores. Pitfall: too few comparisons — Elo needs enough matchups to stabilize, or the ranking is noise.
exercise.goal: Rank models with comparative eval.
steps: Run pairwise comparisons on a task set.
Compute Elo / build a mini arena.
Produce a ranked scorecard.
acceptance: An Elo/ranked scorecard across ≥3 models exists.
Confidence/agreement is noted.
exercise.stretch: Add bootstrap confidence intervals to the Elo.

days.focusSub: capability, cost/latency; build vs buy; benchmarks

resources.label: Huyen Ch 4 — Evaluation Criteria + Model Selection

days.hands: Build a model-selection scorecard for billpay (capability × cost × latency × control). Critically read 2 public benchmarks for leakage/mismatch.

exercise.summary: Model choice should be a scored decision across capability, cost, and latency — not a vibe. You'll score candidates on those axes, decide build-vs-buy for your task, and cross-check against a public benchmark. An explicit scorecard makes the tradeoff defensible. Beginner trap: optimizing only for capability — the strongest model can be too slow or too expensive for your actual budget.
exercise.goal: Select a model on criteria, not vibes.
steps: Score candidates on capability, cost, latency.
Decide build vs buy for your task.
Cross-check against a public benchmark.
acceptance: A selection matrix yields a justified pick.
Benchmark caveats are noted.
exercise.stretch: Recompute the pick if the budget halves.

days.focusSub: eval all components, guidelines, methods + data

resources.label: Huyen Ch 4 — Design Your Evaluation Pipeline

days.hands: Design an end-to-end eval pipeline for your capstone (component-level + system-level); wire it into CI as a release gate.

exercise.summary: This designs the whole eval pipeline — per-component and end-to-end — that runs automatically. You'll define evals for retrieval, the judge, and end-to-end behaviour, write guidelines and methods with the data each needs, and wire it to run on a schedule and on PRs. A pipeline catches regressions you'd never notice by hand. Pitfall: only evaluating end to end — when it fails you won't know which component broke; test the pieces too.
exercise.goal: Design the full eval pipeline.
steps: Define evals for each component (retrieval, judge, end-to-end).
Write guidelines + methods + the data each needs.
Wire it to run on a schedule + on PRs.
acceptance: Every component has an eval defined.
The pipeline runs automatically.
exercise.stretch: Add a regression dashboard over time.

weeks.udemy: Spine: Huyen Ch 3–4 · Maven AI Evals if investing
weeks.deliverable: Repo week-15-eval — perplexity calc + validated judge + Elo + model scorecard + CI eval gate.

weeks.title: Capstone — Billpay Ops Copilot
weeks.goal: Goal: ship one real, end-to-end agentic system you'd demo to a hiring manager. Uses everything: structured outputs, RAG, multi-framework agents, MCP, evals, Langfuse, and Temporal orchestration. No tutorials — just build. Build it JVM-native — Quarkus + Quarkus-LangChain4j + the Temporal Java SDK, reusing your Week 4 work. The Python weeks were for learning; the capstone ships on your stack, which makes it a far stronger portfolio piece for your background.

days.hands: Design doc (architecture, tradeoffs); implement the happy-path diagnosis on one failure class.
Wire Temporal API tool, Oracle read MCP, runbook RAG, metrics tool.
Temporal orchestration, retries/timeouts, HITL approval signal, Langfuse tracing.
Deploy to Fly.io / Modal (full AWS deploy comes in Week 19). Smoke test end-to-end.
≥10 golden incidents in the eval suite; 1500-word writeup on architecture, tradeoffs, "what I'd do at 10× scale."
Pin repo; blog post; LinkedIn + Loom demo. Resume bullets that lead with outcomes ("cut RAG P95 800ms→180ms via hybrid + reranker"), not tech names.
Subscribe: Hamel, Eugene Yan, Latent Space. Open Chip Huyen's book — Part II starts.

weeks.udemy: Synthesis — Agentic Track Capstone + Core Track Wk 8 for reference patterns.
weeks.deliverable: Deployed Billpay Ops Copilot + eval suite + writeup + portfolio assets.

specs.heading: The build — recommended pick (strongest for your portfolio)
specs.body: An agent that diagnoses a stuck Temporal workflow given a workflow ID, then proposes (and optionally executes, behind a human-approval signal) a mitigation. It sits exactly at the intersection of your strengths and AI engineering.
bullets: Input: a workflow ID (or an alert webhook).

Tools / MCP servers: query Temporal API (history, pending activities, stuck task queues); read Oracle business state via your Week-6 read-only MCP server; RAG over runbooks + Temporal docs (Week 6); a metrics tool (shard latency, sync-match rate).

Reasoning: a Quarkus-LangChain4j agent (or LangGraph if you'd rather stay in Python) classifies the failure (sticky-queue starvation, poller starvation, IO:XactSync contention, prepared-statement pinning, …) and drafts a mitigation.
Orchestration: the whole diagnosis runs as a Temporal workflow (Java SDK) — durable, retryable, with a human-approval Signal before any write action.
Quality gates: eval suite (Week 13) with golden incidents; LLM-as-judge on diagnosis correctness.
Observability: full Langfuse tracing of every tool call and token.

specs.heading: Alternative picks (if you want a change of domain)
bullets: Code Review Agent — reviews Kotlin PRs against your team style guide + anti-patterns; RAG over the guide + history.
India Relocation Concierge — multi-step planning (schools, neighborhoods, doc checklists). Personally useful, less technically flashy.
Wildlife Photography Assistant — RAG over EXIF + field guides; suggests lens/settings. Niche but distinctive.

weeks.title: AI System Design
weeks.goal: Goal — bridge from "can build" to "can architect." At top product companies the system-design loop is gatekeeping. Practice 4 canonical AI system designs end-to-end (RAG search, AI coding assistant, recsys + LLMs, agentic at scale), then a mock interview. Your JVM / Temporal / inference-opt edges become concrete answers here, not bullet points.

resources.label: Eugene Yan — System Design for ML
Chip Huyen — Designing ML Systems (intro / Ch 1)
Jay Alammar / others — AI system design write-ups

days.hands: Write your own 7-step AI-systems template: (1) requirements → (2) functional & non-functional → (3) data & eval → (4) model & retrieval → (5) serving / scale / cost → (6) safety & failure modes → (7) iteration & metrics. Use it for every design below.

exercise.summary: AI system-design interviews reward a repeatable structure; this builds yours. You'll write a 7-step template (requirements → functional/non-functional → data and eval → model and retrieval → serving/scale/cost → safety/failure → iteration/metrics), pressure-test it on a toy prompt, and keep it to one page. A template stops you freezing on a blank whiteboard. Beginner trap: jumping straight to architecture — interviewers want requirements and tradeoffs first.
exercise.goal: Build a repeatable AI system-design template.
steps: Write your 7-step template (requirements → functional/non-functional → data & eval → model & retrieval → serving/scale/cost → safety/failure → iteration/metrics).
Pressure-test it on a toy prompt.
Keep it to one page.
acceptance: A reusable 7-step template exists.
It's been applied once end-to-end.
exercise.stretch: Add a checklist of numbers to always state (latency, recall, cost).

days.focusSub: "Design GitHub Code Search with LLM answers"
days.hands: 2-page design doc: index size (≈100M chunks), freshness SLO, recall target, latency budget (p95 < 800ms), hybrid retrieval, reranker placement, caching, multi-tenant isolation, eval pipeline. Put real numbers.

exercise.summary: This is a full design for RAG search over ~100M chunks, with real numbers. You'll write a 2-page design for "GitHub Code Search with LLM answers", commit to a freshness Service-Level Objective (SLO), a recall target, p95 under 800ms, caching, and multi-tenant isolation, and show the eval pipeline. Concrete numbers separate a real design from hand-waving. Pitfall: ignoring freshness and isolation — at scale, stale indexes and tenant data leaks are the hard parts, not the happy path.
exercise.goal: Design LLM-powered search at scale.
steps: Write a 2-page design for 'GitHub Code Search with LLM answers' (~100M chunks).
Put real numbers: freshness SLO, recall target, p95 < 800ms, caching, multi-tenant isolation.
Show the eval pipeline.
acceptance: The doc has concrete numbers, not hand-waving.
Hybrid retrieval + reranker placement are justified.
exercise.stretch: Add a cost-per-query estimate.

resources.label: Cursor / Continue / Aider engineering blog posts

days.hands: Design doc: autocomplete vs chat vs agent modes, context windowing strategy, repo indexing, latency (TTFT < 150ms for autocomplete), local vs cloud model split, privacy, telemetry / eval.

exercise.summary: Designing a Cursor/Copilot-style assistant forces latency and context tradeoffs to the surface. You'll cover autocomplete vs chat vs agent modes, specify context windowing and repo indexing with Time To First Token (TTFT) under 150ms for autocomplete, and address the local-vs-cloud split, privacy, and telemetry. Autocomplete latency is a brutal constraint that shapes everything. Beginner trap: using one big model for autocomplete — it's too slow; fast inline completion needs a small, specialized model.
exercise.goal: Design an AI coding assistant.
steps: Design Cursor/Copilot: autocomplete vs chat vs agent modes.
Specify context windowing, repo indexing, TTFT < 150ms for autocomplete.
Cover local vs cloud split, privacy, telemetry/eval.
acceptance: Latency budgets per mode are stated.
The context/indexing strategy is concrete.
exercise.stretch: Add an offline-degradation story.

days.hands: Design doc: candidate generation (collaborative + content embeddings), LLM reranker at the head (cost gate), feature store, cold-start, online learning loop, A/B + Elo for ranker upgrades.

exercise.summary: This designs a recommender system (RecSys) that uses a model as a reranking feature, not the whole engine. You'll design Spotify Discover with candidate generation plus an LLM reranking head behind a cost gate, cover the feature store, cold-start, and online learning, and specify A/B plus Elo for ranker upgrades. The model is a feature, not the system. Pitfall: putting a model in the hot path for every item — it's too slow and costly; gate it to a small reranked candidate set.
exercise.goal: Design a recsys with LLM features.
steps: Design Spotify Discover with an LLM reranker: candidate generation + reranking head (cost gate).
Cover feature store, cold-start, online learning.
Specify A/B + Elo for ranker upgrades.
acceptance: Candidate-gen vs rerank split is justified on cost.
Cold-start + evaluation are addressed.
exercise.stretch: Estimate the LLM-rerank cost ceiling at scale.

days.focusSub: "Customer-support copilot at 1M tickets / day" — your Temporal lane
days.hands: Design doc: tool inventory, HITL escalation, durable orchestration via Temporal, throttled fan-out (reusing W10 math), inference tier sizing (reusing W18), cost-per-ticket target, failure taxonomy + eval, audit/compliance. Explicitly call out the JVM / Temporal / inference-opt edges.

exercise.summary: This designs an agentic support copilot at 1M tickets/day, pulling together everything: tools, human-in-the-loop escalation, durable orchestration, throttled fan-out, and an inference tier. You'll set a cost-per-ticket target and add a failure taxonomy plus audit/compliance, leaning on your JVM/Temporal/inference edges. Scale turns each component into a real constraint. Beginner trap: designing for the happy path — at this volume, escalation, retries, and cost-per-ticket are the design, not afterthoughts.
exercise.goal: Design an agentic workflow at 1M tickets/day.
steps: Design a support copilot: tool inventory, HITL escalation, durable orchestration (Temporal), throttled fan-out (reuse W10), inference tier (reuse W18).
Set a cost-per-ticket target.
Add a failure taxonomy + audit/compliance — call out your JVM/Temporal/inference edges.
acceptance: The design hits a cost-per-ticket target with numbers.
Your differentiated edges are explicit in the doc.
exercise.stretch: Add a graceful-degradation plan when the model provider is down.

resources.label: Alex Xu — System Design Interview (Vol 2) chapters on ML

days.hands: Record a 45-min mock for one of designs 2–5 (whiteboard or Excalidraw, talk aloud). Score against the rubric: requirements gathering, tradeoffs, depth on bottleneck, eval strategy, your edges. Write a 1-pager: "my interview-ready angles" — JVM-native AI, Temporal orchestration, inference economics.

exercise.summary: Design skill only shows under interview pressure, so you rehearse one out loud on a timer. You'll record a 45-minute mock for one of your designs, score yourself against a rubric (requirements, tradeoffs, bottleneck depth, eval, edges), and write a one-pager of your interview-ready angles. The recording reveals filler and gaps you can't feel live. Pitfall: practicing silently — talking through tradeoffs aloud is a different, harder skill than thinking them.
exercise.goal: Rehearse the 45-minute design interview.
steps: Record a 45-min mock for one of designs 1–4 (whiteboard/Excalidraw, talk aloud).
Score yourself against the rubric (requirements, tradeoffs, bottleneck depth, eval, edges).
Write a 1-pager: 'my interview-ready angles'.
acceptance: A recorded mock exists, self-scored.
The differentiator one-pager is written.
exercise.stretch: Do a second mock and close your weakest rubric area.

weeks.udemy: Spine: Eugene Yan / Huyen / Anthropic posts. No single course fits — interview prep itself is in W19.
weeks.deliverable: Repo week-17-sysdesign — 4 design docs + 1 mock recording + differentiator one-pager.

weeks.title: Inference Optimization
weeks.goal: Goal — YOUR SECOND EDGE. Distributed-systems thinking applied to LLM serving: throughput, batching, caching, latency-vs-cost. This is the strongest specialised material in the plan — most AI engineers can't reason about it; you do this for a living. Huyen Ch 9.

days.focusSub: TTFT, TPOT, throughput, goodput; GPU basics
days.hands: Benchmark TTFT / TPOT / throughput of your W11 model under load; build a latency-vs-cost table.

exercise.summary: Serving models is a systems problem with its own metrics; this measures them properly. You'll benchmark Time To First Token (TTFT), Time Per Output Token (TPOT), and throughput under load, define goodput for your SLO, and build a latency-vs-cost table. These numbers drive every serving decision. Beginner trap: quoting average latency — tail latency (p95/p99) under load is what users feel and what your SLO must target.
exercise.goal: Measure inference like a systems engineer.
steps: Benchmark TTFT / TPOT / throughput of your W11 model under load.
Define goodput for your SLO.
Build a latency-vs-cost table.
acceptance: TTFT/TPOT/throughput are measured under real load.
A latency-vs-cost table exists.
exercise.stretch: Plot throughput vs batch size and find the knee.

days.hands: Enable speculative decoding in vLLM; measure the speedup vs baseline.

exercise.summary: This optimizes the model itself for faster inference. You'll enable speculative decoding in vLLM and measure the speedup, quantize and re-measure, and note any quality delta. Speed gains are only real if quality holds. Pitfall: shipping an optimization without re-checking quality — speculative decoding and quantization can subtly change outputs.
exercise.goal: Optimise the model for inference.
steps: Enable speculative decoding in vLLM and measure speedup.
Quantize and re-measure.
Note any quality delta.
acceptance: Spec-decoding speedup vs baseline is measured.
Quality impact of quantization is recorded.
exercise.stretch: Try tensor parallelism across 2 GPUs (or simulate) and note scaling.

days.focusSub: continuous batching, KV cache, prefill/decode disaggregation
days.hands: Load-test vLLM continuous batching; chart throughput vs concurrency; observe KV-cache + prefill effects.

exercise.summary: Beyond the model, the serving layer's batching and caching decide throughput. You'll load-test vLLM continuous batching, chart throughput vs concurrency, observe Key/Value (KV) cache and prefill effects, and find the concurrency that maximizes goodput. The right concurrency is discovered, not guessed. Beginner trap: maxing concurrency for throughput — past a point latency spikes and goodput (useful, on-time responses) actually falls.
exercise.goal: Optimise the serving layer.
steps: Load-test vLLM continuous batching; chart throughput vs concurrency.
Observe KV-cache + prefill effects.
Find the concurrency that maximises goodput.
acceptance: A throughput-vs-concurrency chart exists.
KV-cache/prefill behaviour is described.
exercise.stretch: Test prefill/decode disaggregation and compare.

days.focusSub: prompt cache, semantic cache, model gateway/router
days.hands: Add prompt + semantic caching; route easy queries to a small model via a gateway; measure $ saved.

exercise.summary: Most production model cost is avoidable through caching and routing. You'll add prompt and semantic caching, route easy queries to a small model via a gateway (LiteLLM), and measure dollars saved on a realistic traffic mix. Not every query needs your most expensive model. Pitfall: semantic cache hits that are "close but wrong" — tune the similarity threshold, or you'll serve a cached answer to a subtly different question.
exercise.goal: Cut cost with caching and routing.
steps: Add prompt + semantic caching.
Route easy queries to a small model via a gateway (LiteLLM).
Measure $ saved on a realistic traffic mix.
acceptance: Cache hit-rate and $ saved are measured.
Routing sends easy traffic to the cheap model correctly.
exercise.stretch: Add a cache-invalidation policy and test it.

days.hands: Design the serving tier for 500K throttled workflows: concurrency caps, batching, cache-hit targets — without scaling the r8g.8xlarge. This is your interview centerpiece.

exercise.summary: This designs the serving tier for a large throttled workload end to end. You'll design concurrency caps, batching, and cache-hit targets for a 500K-workflow fan-out, stay within a fixed Postgres budget, and write it up as an interview centerpiece. Designing within a hard resource budget is the realistic version of the problem. Beginner trap: assuming you can just scale the database — the exercise is hitting the target without scaling the bottleneck.
exercise.goal: Design the serving tier for 500K throttled workflows.
steps: Design concurrency caps, batching, and cache-hit targets for the W10 fan-out.
Stay within the r8g.8xlarge Postgres budget.
Write it up as your interview centerpiece.
acceptance: A serving-tier design hits throughput targets within budget.
It ties explicitly to the Temporal fan-out.
exercise.stretch: Compute the $/1K-requests of the final design.

weeks.udemy: Spine: Huyen Ch 9 — no course matches your background here.
weeks.deliverable: Repo week-18-inference — load-test harness + spec-decoding bench + batching chart + gateway/cache + scale design doc.

weeks.title: AI Eng Architecture & Production Deployment
weeks.goal: Goal: assemble everything into a production reference architecture and deploy it to the cloud — the platform-engineering layer that is 70–80% of real production AI work. Huyen Ch 10 + Ed Donner Production Track.

days.focusSub: context → guardrails → router → cache → agents → orchestration
days.hands: Draw your capstone as the full layered architecture; label each layer in your actual code.

exercise.summary: A reference architecture is the layered diagram of your whole system — context, guardrails, router, cache, agents, orchestration — mapped to real code. You'll diagram your capstone as those layers, label each in your actual code, and mark each layer's failure domain. The diagram is how you reason about and explain the system. Pitfall: a pretty diagram that doesn't match the code — if you can't point to each layer in the repo, it's fiction.
exercise.goal: Draw the production reference architecture.
steps: Diagram your capstone as layers: context → guardrails → router → cache → agents → orchestration.
Label each layer in your actual code.
Mark the failure domain of each.
acceptance: A layered diagram maps 1:1 to your code.
Each layer's failure mode is labelled.
exercise.stretch: Add SLOs per layer.

days.hands: Add input + output guardrails to the capstone; red-team with 10 injections; document each defense.

exercise.summary: Production agents need layered defenses against misuse and injection. You'll add input and output guardrails to the capstone, red-team with 10 injections, and document each defense mapped to the OWASP (Open Worldwide Application Security Project) LLM Top 10. Mapping to a known list makes your security story legible. Beginner trap: treating defensive prompting as enough — system-prompt instructions can be overridden; you need real input/output filters too.
exercise.goal: Harden with guardrails + defensive prompting.
steps: Add input + output guardrails to the capstone.
Red-team with 10 injections.
Document each defense (map to OWASP LLM Top 10).
acceptance: Guardrails block the injection set.
Defenses are mapped to OWASP items.
exercise.stretch: Add an allow/deny policy engine in front of tools.

days.focusSub: Docker, AWS Bedrock, SageMaker, Lambda, App Runner, API Gateway
days.hands: Containerize the capstone (Docker); deploy behind API Gateway + Lambda (or App Runner for the long-running agent); call Bedrock as a model provider.

exercise.summary: This gets your app off localhost and onto the cloud. You'll containerize the capstone with Docker, deploy behind API Gateway + Lambda (or App Runner for a long-running agent), and call Bedrock as a model provider. A deployed URL is what makes the work demoable. Pitfall: choosing Lambda for a long-running agent — its timeout will kill multi-minute jobs; match the compute to the workload.
exercise.goal: Deploy to the cloud.
steps: Containerize the capstone with Docker.
Deploy behind API Gateway + Lambda (or App Runner for the long-running agent).
Call Bedrock as a model provider.
acceptance: The app is reachable on a public endpoint.
Bedrock serves at least one call path.
exercise.stretch: Add autoscaling and load-test the endpoint.

days.hands: Terraform your infra; GitHub Actions pipeline that runs the Week-13 eval gate before any deploy. Note the Vercel/GCP/Azure equivalents the Production Track covers.

exercise.summary: Infrastructure as Code (IaC) plus eval-gated continuous integration/continuous delivery (CI/CD) makes deploys reproducible and safe. You'll Terraform your infrastructure and add a GitHub Actions pipeline that runs the eval gate before any deploy, noting the Vercel/GCP/Azure equivalents. Click-ops doesn't reproduce; code does. Beginner trap: deploying without the eval gate in the pipeline — a green build that skips evals can still ship a quality regression.
exercise.goal: Codify infra + eval-gated CI/CD.
steps: Terraform your infrastructure.
GitHub Actions pipeline runs the W14 eval gate before any deploy.
Note the Vercel/GCP/Azure equivalents.
acceptance: terraform apply provisions the stack reproducibly.
A regressing eval blocks deploy.
exercise.stretch: Add a blue/green or canary deploy step.

days.hands: Deploy the multi-agent capstone serverless (Aurora Serverless + SQS); wire Langfuse; add a conversational feedback-capture loop.

exercise.summary: This goes serverless and closes the loop with user feedback. You'll deploy the multi-agent capstone serverless (Aurora Serverless + SQS), wire Langfuse, and add a conversational feedback-capture loop. Feedback is the fuel for the next round of evals. Pitfall: collecting feedback you never use — capture it in a form your eval pipeline can actually consume.
exercise.goal: Go serverless multi-agent with a feedback loop.
steps: Deploy the multi-agent capstone serverless (Aurora Serverless + SQS).
Wire Langfuse.
Add a conversational feedback-capture loop.
acceptance: The serverless deployment handles a real request.
User feedback is captured and stored.
exercise.stretch: Pipe feedback into the eval set automatically.

days.hands: Add a thin SaaS layer (auth, usage metering, subscription tier) in front of the capstone; document the MLOps loop — monitoring, retraining triggers, rollback. Optional but makes the project look commercial.

exercise.summary: This wraps the capstone as a thin software-as-a-service product with real operational maturity. You'll add auth, usage metering, and a subscription tier, document the MLOps (Machine-Learning Operations) loop — monitoring, retraining triggers, rollback — and add a basic admin/usage view. Productizing forces the operational questions a demo skips. Beginner trap: shipping with no rollback plan — when a model update regresses, you need a fast, tested way back.
exercise.goal: Wrap it as a thin SaaS with MLOps maturity.
steps: Add auth, usage metering, and a subscription tier in front of the capstone.
Document the MLOps loop: monitoring, retraining triggers, rollback.
Add a basic admin/usage view.
acceptance: A user can sign up, is metered, and is tier-limited.
The MLOps loop is documented.
exercise.stretch: Add Stripe test-mode billing.

weeks.udemy: Udemy: Production Track + Huyen Ch 10
weeks.deliverable: Capstone deployed to AWS, Terraform-managed, eval-gated CI/CD, Langfuse-instrumented, with a feedback loop.

weeks.title: Multimodal & Generative Media
weeks.goal: Optional — breadth, and personally relevant. The least central to a backend-agentic path, but it's an entire domain (and ties to your photography). Spine: ByteByteGo P5. Skip without guilt if you're optimizing purely for agentic-backend roles.

days.hands: One-pager comparing the four families; where diffusion won and why.

exercise.summary: This maps the families of generative models so you know why diffusion dominates images. You'll write a one-pager comparing VAEs, GANs, autoregressive models, and diffusion, state where diffusion won and why, and note a use-case per family. The map orients you before the deep dive. Pitfall: treating "generative AI" as only language models — image, audio, and video use very different architectures.
exercise.goal: Map the generative model families.
steps: Write a 1-pager comparing VAE, GANs, autoregressive, diffusion.
State where diffusion won and why.
Note one use-case per family.
acceptance: The 1-pager correctly contrasts the four families.
Diffusion's advantages are explained.
exercise.stretch: Add where autoregressive image models are resurging.

days.focusSub: forward/backward process, U-Net vs DiT, sampling
days.hands: Run the HF diffusion-from-scratch notebook; sample at varying step counts and observe quality/latency.

exercise.summary: This builds real understanding of how diffusion models turn noise into images. You'll run a diffusion-from-scratch notebook, sample at varying step counts to watch the quality/latency tradeoff, and contrast U-Net vs DiT backbones. Step count is the cost knob you'll tune constantly. Beginner trap: assuming more steps are always better — past a point quality plateaus while latency keeps climbing.
exercise.goal: Understand diffusion deeply.
steps: Run the HF diffusion-from-scratch notebook.
Sample at varying step counts; observe quality/latency.
Contrast U-Net vs DiT in notes.
acceptance: The notebook runs and you vary the sampler steps.
Quality-vs-latency tradeoff is recorded.
exercise.stretch: Implement a tiny DDPM sampling loop yourself.

days.focusSub: Stable Diffusion / diffusers; FID, CLIP score
days.hands: Generate images with SDXL via diffusers; evaluate a set with CLIP score; try ControlNet for composition control.

exercise.summary: This generates and — crucially — evaluates text-to-image output. You'll generate with SDXL via diffusers, score a set with CLIP score (and note FID), and try ControlNet for composition control. Evaluating image quality is harder and more important than generating it. Pitfall: judging by a few cherry-picked images — use a metric and a fixed prompt set so "better" means something.
exercise.goal: Generate and evaluate text-to-image.
steps: Generate images with SDXL via diffusers.
Evaluate a set with CLIP score (and note FID).
Try ControlNet for composition control.
acceptance: Images are generated and scored with CLIP.
ControlNet changes composition as expected.
exercise.stretch: Build a small prompt→image grid for A/B comparison.

days.focusSub: latent diffusion, DiT for video, T2V systems
days.hands: Read the T2V system design; run an open T2V model on a short clip; note the scaling challenges.

exercise.summary: Text-to-video is the current frontier; this surveys and runs it. You'll read a text-to-video system design (latent diffusion / DiT for video), run an open model on a short clip, and note the scaling challenges. Understanding the constraints matters more than the demo here. Beginner trap: expecting image-quality results — video adds temporal consistency and huge compute cost that current open models struggle with.
exercise.goal: Survey and run text-to-video.
steps: Read a T2V system design (latent diffusion / DiT for video).
Run an open T2V model on a short clip.
Note the scaling challenges.
acceptance: A short clip is generated.
Scaling bottlenecks are summarised.
exercise.stretch: Estimate the compute to double the clip length.

days.hands: Build a small T2I agent that generates concept references / backdrops from a prompt — a useful pre-shoot tool for your portrait work.

exercise.summary: This ties generative media to a real use of yours — a small text-to-image agent for your photography. You'll build an agent that generates concept references from a prompt, add a style/lighting control, and use it to pre-visualize a real shoot. A personal use-case makes the skill stick. Pitfall: a one-shot generator with no controls — useful creative tools need iteration knobs (style, seed, composition), not just a prompt box.
exercise.goal: Build a generation agent tied to your photography.
steps: Build a small T2I agent that generates concept references/backdrops from a prompt.
Add a style/lighting control.
Use it to pre-visualise one real shoot.
acceptance: The agent produces usable concept references.
It's driven by a clean prompt interface.
exercise.stretch: Add a gallery export of the generated set.

weeks.udemy: Spine: ByteByteGo P5 + HF Diffusion Course
weeks.deliverable: Repo week-20-multimodal — diffusion notebook + T2I generation agent.

weeks.title: Interview Prep, Portfolio & Job Search
weeks.goal: Goal — convert what you built into offers. Everything before this week made you a strong AI engineer; this week makes that legible to a hiring loop. STAR stories from the capstone + Temporal + inference work, take-home rehearsal, GitHub polish, blog posts as proof, target-company outreach. Hireable people skip this and leave money on the table.

days.focusSub: 5 stories that cover the loop — your edges first
days.hands: Write 5 STAR stories (Situation / Task / Action / Result, ≤300 words each): (1) Temporal fan-out at 500K from W10, (2) RAG quality lift from W6/W7, (3) eval-gated CI from W13/W14, (4) JVM-native AI from W4, (5) inference cost/latency win from W18. Each must end with a metric.

exercise.summary: Interviews reward stories, and STAR (Situation, Task, Action, Result) is the structure that lands them. You'll draft five STAR stories from your build (each ≤300 words, ending in a metric), covering your strongest pieces, and lead with your differentiated edges. A metric-ended story beats a vague "I worked on...". Beginner trap: all situation and no result — the Result with a number is the part interviewers remember.
exercise.goal: Write 5 STAR stories from your build.
steps: Draft STAR stories (≤300 words each): Temporal fan-out (W10), RAG lift (W6/W7), eval-gated CI (W13/W14), JVM-native AI (W4), inference win (W18).
End each with a metric.
Lead with your differentiated edges.
acceptance: 5 STAR stories exist, each ending in a metric.
Edge stories (JVM/Temporal/inference) are front-loaded.
exercise.stretch: Record yourself telling two in under 2 minutes each.

days.hands: Find 2 public AI-engineer take-homes ("build a small RAG on this corpus," "design + ship an agent for X"). Do one on a 4-hour timer; ship a working repo with README, evals, and a 1-page writeup. Reflect: where did you go over time, what would you cut next time.

exercise.summary: Take-home assignments are won by scoping under time pressure, which you can rehearse. You'll find two public AI-engineer take-homes, do one on a 4-hour timer shipping a repo with README, evals, and a writeup, then post-mortem where you overran. Practicing the clock is the real skill. Pitfall: gold-plating one feature and skipping evals/README — graders reward a complete, documented solution over a half-finished clever one.
exercise.goal: Rehearse a real take-home under time.
steps: Find 2 public AI-engineer take-homes.
Do one on a 4-hour timer; ship a repo with README, evals, and a 1-page writeup.
Post-mortem where you went over time.
acceptance: A complete, working take-home repo is shipped.
A post-mortem lists what to cut next time.
exercise.stretch: Do the second under 3 hours applying the lessons.

days.focusSub: Recruiters open the repo, not the resume
days.hands: Upgrade the capstone README: architecture diagram (Excalidraw), 60-sec demo gif, eval scoreboard, deploy notes, "what I'd build next." Pin top 5 repos on your profile. Add a one-screen profile README that links the 3 things you want them to read first.

exercise.summary: Recruiters skim, so your GitHub has to sell the work in seconds. You'll upgrade the capstone README with an architecture diagram, a 60-second demo gif, an eval scoreboard, and deploy notes, pin your top five repos, and add a one-screen profile README. Presentation is part of the engineering. Beginner trap: a great project with a bare README — if the first screen doesn't show what it does, most people never run it.
exercise.goal: Polish GitHub + portfolio for recruiters.
steps: Upgrade the capstone README: architecture diagram, 60-sec demo gif, eval scoreboard, deploy notes.
Pin your top 5 repos.
Add a one-screen profile README linking the 3 things to read first.

The profile is curated and pinned.

days.focusSub: The W10 + W18 posts you've been drafting

resources.label: Eugene Yan — Why you should write

days.hands: Publish the W10 post ("What Agent Frameworks Get Wrong About Workflow Orchestration"). Start the W18 post ("Inference Economics for Agentic Workloads"). Cross-post to your site, dev.to, LinkedIn. These are linkable proof in every conversation.

exercise.summary: Published writing is durable proof of expertise that works while you sleep. You'll publish your orchestration post, start an inference-economics post, and cross-post to your site, dev.to, and LinkedIn. A good post is a credential you can link in every application. Pitfall: waiting for the "perfect" post — shipping a solid one and iterating beats an unpublished masterpiece.

steps: Publish the W10 post ('What Agent Frameworks Get Wrong About Workflow Orchestration').
Start the W18 post ('Inference Economics for Agentic Workloads').

resources.label: Gergely Orosz — How to find AI engineering jobs

days.hands: Build a target list of 20 companies in 3 buckets — frontier labs / AI-first product / AI-augmented incumbents. Tailor the resume per bucket. Identify 5 warm intros (LinkedIn 2nd-degree) and send messages today. Track in a simple sheet.

exercise.summary: A job search converts better as a targeted campaign than a spray of applications. You'll list 20 companies in three buckets (frontier labs / AI-first products / AI-augmented incumbents), tailor your resume per bucket, and message 5 warm intros today, tracking it in a sheet. Warm intros convert far better than cold applies. Beginner trap: one generic resume for everyone — tailoring to the bucket is what gets the first reply.

steps: List 20 companies in 3 buckets (frontier labs / AI-first product / AI-augmented incumbents).
Tailor your resume per bucket.
Identify 5 warm intros and message them today; track in a sheet.
acceptance: A 20-company list with 3 buckets + tailored resumes.
5 outreach messages are sent and tracked.

glossary.def: A neural network trained on huge amounts of text to predict and generate language.
Fetching relevant documents and feeding them to a model so its answers are grounded in your own data.
An open standard (from Anthropic) for connecting AI models to external tools and data sources.
A large model pre-trained on broad data that can be adapted to many downstream tasks.
Training a base model on curated input→output examples to teach a desired behaviour.
A family of methods (including LoRA) that adapt a model by training only a small fraction of its parameters.
A fine-tuning method that trains small adapter matrices instead of the full model, saving memory.
LoRA applied on top of a quantized (compressed) base model, enabling fine-tuning on a single GPU.
Aligning a model to human preferences using a reward model learned from human comparisons.
A simpler alternative to RLHF that optimizes preferences directly, without a separate reward model.
Fine-tuning a model to use retrieved documents well and ignore distracting ones.
A method where a model bootstraps its own training data from the reasoning chains it gets right.
A reward model that scores each step of a reasoning chain, not just the final answer.
An agent pattern that interleaves chain-of-thought reasoning with tool-use actions.
An agent pattern that plans all tool calls up front to cut token usage.
A workflow where a person reviews or approves an AI action before it proceeds.
A graph-based index for fast approximate nearest-neighbour search in vector databases.
A vector-search index that clusters vectors into lists to speed up approximate search.
A classic keyword-ranking function for text search — lexical, not semantic.
A way to merge several ranked lists (e.g. keyword + vector results) into one.
A retrieval metric: the average of 1 / (rank of the first relevant result).
A retrieval trick: generate a hypothetical answer, embed it, and search with that.
How long a model takes to produce its first output token — the latency a user feels first.
The average time to generate each token after the first.
Stored attention keys and values that let a model skip recomputing past tokens, speeding up generation.
The memory on a GPU — the main limit on which models you can run or train.
A file format for distributing quantized models for local inference (e.g. llama.cpp).
A post-training method that compresses model weights to 3–4 bits.
A tokenization algorithm that builds a vocabulary by merging frequent character pairs.
A model that learns shared embeddings for images and text, enabling text↔image search.
A target for a service metric (e.g. 99% of requests under 2s) used to manage reliability.
Managing servers and cloud resources through version-controlled config files (e.g. Terraform).
Automated pipelines that test and ship code changes safely and often.
Practices and tooling for deploying, monitoring, and maintaining ML systems in production.
A Git-like tool for versioning datasets and machine-learning pipelines.
A nonprofit publishing widely-used security guidance, including a Top 10 for LLM apps.
A system that predicts and suggests items a user is likely to want.
A structure for answering behavioural interview questions with one concrete story.
The runtime that executes Java, Kotlin, and other bytecode languages.
A library plus tools for building on top of a platform or service.
Amazon's cloud platform.
Google's cloud platform.
